deck.title: CCNP ENCOR 350-401 Flashcards
deck.description: Flashcards aligned to the Cisco ENCOR 350-401 exam blueprint.

front.text: In MQC, what is the difference between a class-map and a policy-map?

back.text: A class-map identifies traffic. A policy-map applies actions to that traffic, such as marking, policing, shaping, queueing, or dropping.

class-map match-any VOICE
 match dscp ef
!
policy-map WAN-QOS
 class VOICE
  priority percent 10

front.text: What is the key operational difference between policing and shaping?
Why does OSPF network type matter?

back.text: OSPF network type affects neighbor discovery, DR/BDR election behavior, adjacency formation, and how LSAs are flooded across the segment.
For ENCOR, be able to compare broadcast and point-to-point behavior.

front.text: What happens when an OSPF-enabled interface is configured as passive?

back.text: The network is still advertised into OSPF, but the router does not send or receive OSPF hellos on that interface, so no neighbor adjacency forms there.

router ospf 1
 passive-interface GigabitEthernet0/1

front.text: What must be true for directly connected eBGP neighbors to form?

back.text: The routers must have IP reachability to each other, correct neighbor statements, matching remote AS values, and TCP/179 connectivity.

front.text: Name three early BGP best-path attributes in Cisco path selection order.

items: Highest weight
Highest local preference
Locally originated route
Shortest AS path

front.text: Compare the three QoS models: Best-Effort, IntServ, and DiffServ.

back.text: DiffServ is the model used in nearly all modern enterprise QoS designs.

front.text: What is the difference between classification and marking?

back.text: Classification identifies and groups traffic (using ACLs, NBAR, DSCP, etc.) so QoS policy can act on it. Marking writes a value (DSCP, CoS, MPLS EXP) into the packet/frame so downstream devices can trust it without re-classifying.

front.text: What DSCP values are commonly used for voice, video, and signaling?
Where is the CoS (802.1p) field located, and how many bits is it?

back.text: CoS is a 3-bit field in the 802.1Q VLAN tag (PCP bits). It allows 8 priority values (0–7) and is only present on tagged Ethernet frames, so it is lost on access ports or when frames cross a Layer 3 boundary.

front.text: What is a QoS trust boundary and where should it be placed?

back.text: The trust boundary is the point in the network where QoS markings from external devices are accepted as valid. It should be placed as close to the source as possible — typically the access switch port — and ideally only trust markings from known endpoints like Cisco IP phones via CDP/LLDP.

front.text: List the three MQC steps required to apply QoS to an interface.

items: Define a class-map to match traffic
Define a policy-map that references the class-map and applies actions
Apply the policy-map to an interface with service-policy input|output

front.text: What does the token bucket represent in a policer?

back.text: The token bucket models the allowed burst. Tokens are added at the configured rate (CIR) and consumed by transmitted bytes. If enough tokens exist, traffic conforms; if not, traffic exceeds (and is dropped or remarked). Bucket depth (Bc) defines the maximum burst tolerated.

front.text: Compare single-rate three-color (srTCM) and two-rate three-color (trTCM) policers.
Show how to shape all traffic in a class to 10 Mbps.

back.text:
policy-map WAN-OUT
 class class-default
  shape average 10000000
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0
 service-policy output WAN-OUT
shape average uses CIR only; shape peak allows bursting above CIR up to PIR.

front.text: What is Low Latency Queuing (LLQ) and why is it used for voice?

back.text: LLQ is CBWFQ plus a strict-priority queue. The priority queue is serviced first to guarantee low delay/jitter for voice, but is policed at its configured rate to prevent starving other queues.

policy-map WAN-OUT
 class VOICE
  priority percent 10
 class class-default
  fair-queue

front.text: How does CBWFQ allocate bandwidth between user-defined classes?

back.text: Each class gets a guaranteed minimum bandwidth (using bandwidth, bandwidth percent, or bandwidth remaining percent). During congestion, classes share the link proportionally; during non-congestion, any class can exceed its share.

front.text: What problem does WRED solve compared to tail drop?

back.text: Tail drop discards all arriving packets once a queue is full, causing TCP global synchronization where many flows back off and ramp up together. WRED probabilistically drops packets before the queue is full, based on IP precedence/DSCP, to throttle TCP senders earlier and avoid synchronized behavior.

front.text: What is the difference between match-any and match-all in a class-map?

back.text: match-all (default) requires every match statement to be true (logical AND). match-any requires only one match statement to be true (logical OR).

class-map match-any VOICE
 match dscp ef
 match dscp cs5

front.text: What does NBAR2 provide for QoS classification?

back.text: NBAR2 is a deep packet inspection engine that identifies applications by signatures, not just port numbers. It enables protocol-based classification (e.g., match protocol webex-meeting) and is the basis for application-aware QoS in Cisco SD-WAN and traditional MQC.

front.text: Which command shows live QoS policy statistics on an interface?

back.text: show policy-map interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0
Displays per-class match counts, offered/drop rates, queue depth, and policer/shaper conform/exceed bytes.

front.text: A Cisco IP phone is connected to an access switch with a PC behind it. How should the trust boundary be configured?

back.text: Trust the markings coming from the IP phone (extended via CDP/LLDP), but have the phone re-mark or zero out CoS/DSCP values from the attached PC. This pushes the trust boundary to the phone, not the untrusted PC.

interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5
 switchport mode access
 switchport voice vlan 110
 auto qos voip cisco-phone

front.text: What is a common use case for hierarchical (nested) QoS policy-maps?

back.text: Shape an aggregate to a sub-line rate (e.g., a 10 Mbps subscribed rate on a Gig interface), then apply a child policy that does CBWFQ/LLQ inside that shaped rate. This is essential on WAN/MPLS handoffs where the physical and logical rates differ.

policy-map CHILD
 class VOICE
  priority percent 10
 class class-default
  fair-queue
!
policy-map PARENT
 class class-default
  shape average 10000000
  service-policy CHILD

front.text: Why can DSCP values be lost or remapped at a Layer 2 trunk boundary?

back.text: CoS lives in the 802.1Q tag and DSCP lives in the IP header. On a switch with a CoS-based queueing model, DSCP is mapped to CoS using DSCP-to-CoS tables. Because CoS is only 3 bits vs 6 bits of DSCP, multiple DSCP values map to the same CoS, so granularity can be lost. Untagged frames lose CoS entirely.

front.text: Write a policy-map that classifies HTTP traffic and marks it AF21.

back.text:
class-map match-any WEB
 match protocol http
!
policy-map MARK-IN
 class WEB
  set dscp af21
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 service-policy input MARK-IN

front.text: Why does the LLQ priority queue have an implicit policer?

back.text: Without a built-in rate limiter, a priority class could starve all other queues during congestion. The implicit policer drops priority traffic that exceeds the configured rate when the link is congested, protecting non-priority classes while still giving voice strict-priority service.

front.text: Why must the tx-ring be small for software queueing (CBWFQ/LLQ) to be effective?

back.text: Software queues only engage when the hardware tx-ring is full (interface congested). If the tx-ring is too large, packets bypass software queues and QoS scheduling has no effect. A small tx-ring forces congestion back into the IOS software queues where MQC policy is applied.

front.text: Voice users complain of choppy audio across a WAN link. show policy-map interface shows drops in the priority class. What are likely causes?

items: Priority bandwidth is set too low for actual call volume
Voice traffic is not being marked correctly (not matching class-map)
Shaper parent rate is below the contracted CIR causing buffer/policer drops
Tx-ring too large, so QoS isn't engaging
Provider is re-marking or policing on ingress to the SP cloud

front.text: What does AutoQoS do?

back.text: AutoQoS generates a Cisco-recommended baseline QoS configuration (class-maps, policy-maps, trust settings, and queueing) with a single command. It is a starting point for VoIP and enterprise traffic and can be customized after generation.

front.text: Define Per-Hop Behavior (PHB) in DiffServ.

back.text: A PHB is the externally observable forwarding treatment a DiffServ-compliant node applies to a packet based on its DSCP value. Standard PHBs include Default (DF), Class Selector (CS), Assured Forwarding (AF), and Expedited Forwarding (EF).

front.text: In Assured Forwarding (e.g., AF31, AF32, AF33), what does the second digit mean?

back.text: The first digit is the AF class (queue/bandwidth), and the second digit is the drop precedence within the class. Higher second digit = higher drop probability under congestion. So AF33 is dropped before AF31 in the same queue.

front.text: Configure a policer that conforms at 5 Mbps and remarks exceeding traffic to DSCP AF11.

back.text:
policy-map LIMIT-WEB
 class WEB
  police 5000000
   conform-action transmit
   exceed-action set-dscp-transmit af11

front.text: Which QoS actions are valid on ingress vs egress?
Why might DSCP appear correctly on outbound voice but show as 0 on the return path?

back.text: Markings are not automatically symmetric. The remote site or service provider may not honor or re-mark DSCP, ingress ACLs/policies on intermediate devices may zero markings, or the trust boundary on the return path may not extend to the source. Verify with show policy-map interface and packet captures on both directions.

front.text: What is the difference between the bandwidth and priority commands in a policy-map?
What does this output tell you?

Class-map: VOICE (match-any)
  500000 packets, 96000000 bytes
  5 minute offered rate 800000 bps, drop rate 12000 bps
  Match: dscp ef
  Queueing
    Strict Priority
    Output Queue: Conversation 264
    Bandwidth 10 (%)
    Bandwidth 1000 (kbps) Burst 25000 (Bytes)
    (pkts matched/bytes matched) 500000/96000000
    (total drops/bytes drops) 600/115200

back.text: VOICE is a strict-priority (LLQ) class matching DSCP EF, configured for 10% (1 Mbps). Offered rate is 800 kbps — under the limit — but there are 600 drops, suggesting micro-bursts above 1 Mbps that hit the implicit priority policer. Consider increasing the priority bandwidth or burst, or verify that other classes aren't forcing congestion.

front.text: Define jitter and explain why it matters for voice traffic.

back.text: Jitter is the variation in delay between successive packets. Voice codecs expect packets to arrive at fixed intervals (e.g., every 20 ms). High jitter forces the receiver's de-jitter buffer to grow, increasing latency, or to drop late packets, causing audio gaps.
Targets for VoIP: <150 ms one-way delay, <30 ms jitter, <1% loss.

front.text: What are the four components of end-to-end network delay?

items: Processing delay — time to examine the packet header and make a forwarding decision
Queueing delay — time waiting in an interface queue (variable, controllable by QoS)
Serialization delay — time to clock bits onto the wire (depends on link speed)
Propagation delay — time for the signal to travel the medium (distance/speed of light)

front.text: What is the MPLS EXP field and how does it relate to DSCP?

back.text: The MPLS EXP (now called Traffic Class) is a 3-bit field in the MPLS label header used by MPLS LSRs for QoS treatment. Because it is only 3 bits, DSCP values must be mapped (often via Class Selector or Pipe/Short-Pipe/Uniform tunneling models) when packets enter the MPLS domain.

front.text: Compare the Uniform, Pipe, and Short-Pipe DiffServ tunneling models in MPLS.

rows: Copied from MPLS EXP at PE
Based on EXP on PE-CE link
Based on customer DSCP on egress

front.text: What policy-map actions are used to mark CoS, DSCP, and IP precedence?

back.text:
policy-map MARK
 class VOICE
  set dscp ef
  set cos 5
  set precedence 5
set dscp writes 6 bits in the IP header DS field; set cos writes 3 bits in the 802.1Q tag; set precedence writes the legacy 3-bit IP precedence.

front.text: What is class-default in a policy-map?

back.text: class-default is an implicit catch-all class that matches any traffic not matched by user-defined classes. By default it has no guaranteed bandwidth and is FIFO, but you can apply fair-queue, bandwidth, shape, or WRED to it.

front.text: When should you use bandwidth remaining percent instead of bandwidth percent?

back.text: Use bandwidth remaining percent when LLQ priority queues are present. The priority queue's bandwidth is subtracted first, and bandwidth remaining percent allocates the leftover capacity proportionally — preventing oversubscription errors and adapting if the priority class size changes.

policy-map WAN
 class VOICE
  priority percent 10
 class BUSINESS
  bandwidth remaining percent 60
 class class-default
  bandwidth remaining percent 40

front.text: How do you configure a Catalyst switch port to trust the incoming DSCP value?

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet1/0/10
 switchport mode access
 auto qos trust dscp
On older platforms, mls qos trust dscp under the interface; on newer IOS-XE switches, MQC service-policies replace the legacy mls qos commands.

front.text: What is conditional trust on a Catalyst access port?

back.text: Conditional trust extends the trust boundary only when a specific device (e.g., Cisco IP phone) is detected via CDP/LLDP. If the phone is unplugged or replaced by a non-trusted device, the port automatically reverts to untrusted, preventing PCs from sending self-marked priority traffic.

front.text: What does a 1P7Q3T egress queue structure mean on a Catalyst switch?

back.text: 1P7Q3T = 1 strict-priority queue + 7 normal queues, each with 3 WRED drop thresholds. Priority is serviced first; the other queues are scheduled by weight. The 3 thresholds let WRED drop lower-priority DSCP values from a queue earlier than higher-priority values.

front.text: What is RFC 4594 and why is it relevant?

back.text: RFC 4594 is the IETF guideline for DiffServ service classes, defining a standardized 12-class model (e.g., VoIP=EF, Broadcast Video=CS5, Multimedia Conferencing=AF4x, Network Control=CS6). Cisco's Enterprise QoS designs are based on it.

front.text: An access port is set to trust CoS, but voice frames consistently show CoS 0 in queueing stats. What's happening?

back.text: Access ports send untagged frames, so there is no 802.1Q tag and no CoS field to trust. Either trust DSCP instead, or use a voice VLAN so the phone sends 802.1Q-tagged frames carrying a valid CoS value.

front.text: Why are link fragmentation and interleaving (LFI) used on slow WAN links?

back.text: On slow links (<768 kbps), serialization of a large data packet can introduce excessive delay/jitter for voice. LFI fragments large packets and interleaves voice packets between the fragments, reducing serialization delay and protecting voice quality.

front.text: Which commands display class-map and policy-map definitions on a router?

back.text:
show class-map
show class-map VOICE
show policy-map
show policy-map WAN-OUT
These show configured definitions only. Use show policy-map interface to see live counters and runtime behavior.

front.text: When traffic enters a GRE or IPsec tunnel, what happens to DSCP markings?

back.text: Cisco IOS by default copies the inner IP DSCP value to the outer (tunnel) IP header so transit devices honor QoS. With IPsec, qos pre-classify can also be enabled so QoS policies see the original (pre-encryption) classification fields.

front.text: What does qos pre-classify do, and where is it configured?

back.text: qos pre-classify allows a QoS service-policy on a tunnel/crypto interface to classify based on the original (pre-encryption/encapsulation) packet header, instead of just the post-encryption outer header. It is configured under the tunnel or crypto map.

interface Tunnel0
 qos pre-classify

front.text: What is the difference between WRR and SRR scheduling on Catalyst switches?
How do WRED min-threshold, max-threshold, and mark probability denominator (MPD) interact?

back.text: Below min-threshold no packets are dropped. Between min and max, drop probability rises linearly up to 1/MPD at max-threshold. Above max-threshold all packets are dropped (tail drop).

front.text: Why are voice bearer and voice signaling marked with different DSCP values?

back.text: Voice bearer (RTP) is delay-sensitive and gets EF (DSCP 46) for strict-priority queueing. Signaling (SIP, SCCP, H.323) tolerates more delay but must be reliable, so it gets CS3 (DSCP 24) in a guaranteed-bandwidth class. Mixing them in EF would waste priority bandwidth.

front.text: What is ECN and how does it relate to WRED?

back.text: Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN) uses 2 bits in the IP DS field to signal congestion to ECN-capable endpoints instead of dropping packets. With WRED ECN enabled, the router sets the CE (Congestion Experienced) bit instead of dropping when the flow supports ECN, allowing TCP to slow down without retransmission.

front.text: Configure a two-rate three-color policer with CIR 5 Mbps and PIR 10 Mbps.

back.text:
policy-map TWO-RATE
 class DATA
  police cir 5000000 pir 10000000
   conform-action transmit
   exceed-action set-dscp-transmit af11
   violate-action drop

front.text: What is the difference between hardware queueing on a switch and software queueing on a router?
Your SP supports only 4 classes (Real-time, Critical, Business, Best-Effort). Your enterprise uses an 8-class model. What QoS strategy is required at the WAN edge?

back.text: Apply a class-based marking policy on the egress WAN interface that maps the 8 internal DSCP values into the 4 SP-supported values. For example: EF→EF, CS6/CS5/AF4→AF31, AF2/AF3→AF21, default→default. The internal markings stay intact within the enterprise; only the WAN handoff is collapsed.

front.text: Which command shows the queueing strategy and queue depth on a router interface?

back.text:
show queueing interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0
show interfaces GigabitEthernet0/0/0
show interfaces lists the active queueing strategy (FIFO, fair-queue, CBWFQ) and current queue size; show queueing gives more detail per class.

front.text: What is the difference between a color-blind and a color-aware policer?

back.text: A color-blind policer assumes all incoming traffic is green and assigns colors based purely on rate vs CIR/PIR. A color-aware policer respects existing markings — already-marked yellow/red packets cannot be promoted to green even if tokens are available. Color-aware is used when enforcing pre-existing SLA markings.

front.text: How does Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN implement QoS differently from traditional MQC?

back.text: SD-WAN uses centralized policy from vManage. Traffic is matched by application (via SD-AVC/NBAR) into forwarding classes, then mapped to per-tunnel/per-transport queues with built-in shapers. App-aware routing chooses transports based on SLA (loss/latency/jitter) and steers classes onto the best path dynamically — a capability not native to MQC.

front.text: Applying a policy-map fails with 'I/O memory or bandwidth not available'. What does this mean?

back.text: By default, the sum of bandwidth statements in CBWFQ classes (plus priority) cannot exceed 75% of interface bandwidth — the rest is reserved for overhead and routing protocols. Either reduce class allocations, use bandwidth remaining percent, or change max-reserved-bandwidth on the interface.

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 max-reserved-bandwidth 90

front.text: Show how to classify SSH traffic into a class using an ACL.

back.text:
ip access-list extended SSH-TRAFFIC
 permit tcp any any eq 22
!
class-map match-all MGMT
 match access-group name SSH-TRAFFIC
!
policy-map MARK-IN
 class MGMT
  set dscp cs2

front.text: On show interfaces, what's the difference between 'output drops', 'output buffer failures', and 'queue drops' from a QoS perspective?

rows: Packets discarded due to congestion (most often QoS/queue full)
No system buffer available — usually a memory/sizing issue, not pure QoS
From show policy-map interface — exact QoS-level drops per class

back.text: Always correlate show interfaces drops with show policy-map interface to attribute drops to a specific class.

front.text: A 50 Mbps WAN link must support 30 concurrent G.711 calls (~80 kbps each w/ overhead) plus business data. Design a QoS policy outline.

items: Calculate voice bandwidth: 30 calls × ~80 kbps ≈ 2.4 Mbps; allocate ~5% (2.5 Mbps) priority for headroom
Mark voice EF, signaling CS3, business AF31, scavenger CS1 at the access edge
On WAN egress: priority percent 5 for VOICE, bandwidth remaining percent for SIGNALING/BUSINESS/class-default
Add WRED in data classes to prevent TCP synchronization
Apply parent shaper if the contracted CIR is below physical link rate (e.g., 50 Mbps logical on a 1G port)

front.text: How is the IPv4 ToS byte structured in the DiffServ era?

back.text: The legacy IP Precedence used only the top 3 bits, which is why Class Selector DSCP values (CS0–CS7) align with old precedence values.

front.text: Where does QoS marking live in an IPv6 packet?

back.text: IPv6 has an 8-bit Traffic Class field in the fixed header, equivalent to the IPv4 ToS byte: 6 bits DSCP + 2 bits ECN. The same DSCP values (EF, AFxx, CSx) apply identically to IPv6 traffic.

front.text: Why do Class Selector DSCP values exist (CS1–CS7)?

back.text: Class Selector code points provide backward compatibility with legacy IP Precedence. CSx maps to IP Precedence value x (e.g., CS5 = precedence 5 = DSCP 40). This lets old IP-Precedence-aware devices interoperate with DiffServ-aware devices using equivalent treatments.

front.text: What is a qos-group and when is it used?

back.text: qos-group is an internal-only marker (not on the wire) used to carry classification context across IOS subsystems. You set qos-group on ingress, then match qos-group on egress, allowing classification logic to be shared between input and output policies without altering DSCP or CoS.

policy-map IN
 class VOICE
  set qos-group 5
!
class-map match-any QG5
 match qos-group 5
!
policy-map OUT
 class QG5
  priority percent 10

front.text: How do you classify traffic based on the source ingress interface?

back.text:
class-map match-all FROM-DC
 match input-interface GigabitEthernet0/1
!
policy-map WAN-OUT
 class FROM-DC
  bandwidth percent 30
Useful when traffic from a specific VLAN/segment must receive different egress treatment without re-marking.

front.text: What are Bc and Be in a policer, and what is a typical Bc default?

back.text: Bc (committed burst) is the maximum number of bytes that can be sent in a burst at the CIR; Be (excess burst) is additional burst capacity beyond Bc. Cisco's default Bc when not specified is CIR/32 or 1500 bytes (whichever is greater). Too small a Bc causes excessive drops on bursty TCP traffic.

front.text: How does the shaper compute Tc and why does it matter for voice?

back.text: Tc (time interval) = Bc / CIR. The shaper releases Bc bits every Tc. A larger Tc means longer pauses between bursts, increasing jitter for real-time traffic. For voice, target Tc of 10 ms by tuning Bc (Bc = CIR × 0.01).

front.text: Calculate the serialization delay for a 1500-byte packet on a 1.544 Mbps T1 link.

back.text: Serialization = (1500 × 8) / 1,544,000 ≈ 7.77 ms. This is significant for voice (target <10 ms one-hop), and is why LFI is recommended on links below ~768 kbps.

front.text: What does cRTP (Compressed RTP) do and where is it appropriate?

back.text: cRTP compresses the 40-byte IP/UDP/RTP header down to ~2–4 bytes per voice packet on a per-hop basis. It dramatically reduces voice bandwidth on low-speed WAN links but adds CPU cost, so it's used on slow point-to-point links — not on high-speed or multipoint topologies.

front.text: Which command shows the QoS configuration AutoQoS generated on a switch?

back.text:
show auto qos
show auto qos interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5
show run | begin policy-map

front.text: DSCP EF is being correctly set on ingress, but voice still suffers jitter on egress. Where is the likely gap?

back.text: Marking alone doesn't change forwarding. Without an egress service-policy that classifies on DSCP EF and places the traffic into a priority/LLQ queue, EF traffic competes equally with everything else. Apply an output policy-map with priority for class VOICE on the congested egress interface.

front.text: How can you classify traffic based on a specific VLAN ID?

back.text:
class-map match-all VLAN20
 match vlan 20
!
policy-map MARK
 class VLAN20
  set dscp af21

front.text: Why are queueing actions only valid on egress, not ingress?

back.text: Queueing manages contention for an outgoing interface's bandwidth. Ingress traffic doesn't experience that contention until it reaches an egress port, so queue scheduling decisions logically belong at egress. Ingress can still classify, mark, and police.

front.text: How is QoS marking preserved across an SD-Access fabric?

back.text: SD-Access uses VXLAN encapsulation. The original packet's DSCP is copied into the outer (underlay) IP header so underlay devices can still queue based on DSCP. The original DSCP is retained in the inner header and decapsulated at the egress fabric edge.

front.text: Show how to apply the same policy-map at both ingress and egress.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0
 service-policy input EDGE-IN
 service-policy output WAN-OUT
Each direction has its own policy. You cannot apply the same policy-map twice in the same direction on one interface.

front.text: Why is traffic profiling an essential first step before designing QoS?

back.text: You can't allocate bandwidth, set priority, or size queues without knowing what applications run, their volumes, and tolerance for loss/jitter/delay. Tools like NetFlow, NBAR2, and Catalyst Center use profiling data to drive accurate class definitions and bandwidth percentages.

front.text: Configure conditional trust for a Cisco IP phone using AutoQoS.

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet1/0/10
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 10
 switchport voice vlan 110
 auto qos voip cisco-phone
AutoQoS adds class-maps, policy-maps, and conditional trust based on CDP detection of the phone.

front.text: Users report that bulk file transfers stall whenever a video conference is active. What is happening and how do you fix it?

back.text: Video may be in a priority queue with too high a rate, starving non-priority classes. Either move video into a CBWFQ class with bandwidth (not priority), reduce the priority allocation, or use bandwidth remaining percent for fairness in the remaining classes.

front.text: Can a policy-map have more than one priority class, and why would you want that?

back.text: Yes — IOS supports multi-level priority (priority level 1, priority level 2). Voice goes to level 1 (serviced first), interactive video to level 2. Both bypass CBWFQ scheduling, but level 1 is always serviced before level 2, ensuring voice protection even when video is bursty.

policy-map WAN-OUT
 class VOICE
  priority level 1 percent 10
 class VIDEO
  priority level 2 percent 25

front.text: On a Catalyst 9000 series switch, which command exposes hardware queue statistics for an interface?

back.text: show platform hardware fed switch active qos queue stats interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1
Useful when show policy-map interface lacks visibility into ASIC-level queue depths and drops.

front.text: Compare priority percent X with priority X (kbps) in a policy-map.

rows: Fixed absolute rate; doesn't scale with interface speed
Scales with interface bandwidth; same policy reusable on different links
Percent of bandwidth left after other priority/bandwidth classes

front.text: What does this snippet indicate?

GigabitEthernet0/0/0 is up, line protocol is up
  Total output drops: 142039
  Queueing strategy: Class-based queueing
  Output queue: 0/1000/142039 (size/max total/drops)

back.text: The interface is congested and class-based queueing is dropping packets. Use show policy-map interface Gi0/0/0 to see which class(es) are dropping and whether to increase bandwidth, adjust shaping, or add WRED to data classes.

front.text: A class-map matches an ACL with thousands of entries and CPU rises. What's the issue?

back.text: On platforms that classify in software, large ACLs cause linear CPU lookups per packet. Reduce by summarizing the ACL, using DSCP-based matching after the first hop, leveraging NBAR/protocol matching, or matching on object groups so the ACL is hardware-accelerated.

front.text: Your provider gives a 100 Mbps Ethernet handoff but contracts 30 Mbps CIR. You see provider drops at random. What QoS technique fixes this?

back.text: Apply an egress shaper at 30 Mbps so the customer router never bursts above the contracted CIR. Without shaping, the 100 Mbps physical link will burst into the SP, which then polices and drops. Inside the shaper, attach a child policy with LLQ/CBWFQ to prioritize voice/critical traffic within the 30 Mbps.

front.text: What is per-VLAN MQC (VLAN-based QoS) on a Catalyst switch?

back.text: Per-VLAN QoS allows a service-policy to be applied to an SVI or trunk and treat each VLAN independently. It's used when policy must differ per VLAN over a shared trunk — for example, shaping a guest VLAN to 50 Mbps while leaving the corporate VLAN unrestricted on the same physical port.

cards.subtopic: Egress vs ingress policing use case

front.text: When would you police on ingress vs egress?

rows: Drop attack/scavenger traffic early; rate-limit untrusted flows before they consume backplane
Enforce subscriber/SLA contracts going to a WAN/SP; rate-limit per-class within a shaped parent

front.text: Why is DSCP preferred over CoS for end-to-end QoS?

back.text: DSCP rides in the IP header, surviving every routed hop and across L2 boundaries (untagged ports, different VLANs). CoS exists only inside an 802.1Q tag and is lost the moment the frame is untagged or routed. DSCP also offers 64 values vs CoS's 8.

front.text: How do you turn on NBAR protocol discovery on an interface to baseline traffic?

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0
 ip nbar protocol-discovery
!
show ip nbar protocol-discovery
Provides per-protocol byte/packet counts and bit rates — invaluable for sizing CBWFQ classes during QoS design.

front.text: What is the scavenger class in Cisco's enterprise QoS model?

back.text: A class for traffic that is below best-effort — peer-to-peer, bulk backups, or suspected abuse. Marked CS1 (DSCP 8). It receives less than default during congestion and is dropped first. Used to protect business traffic from non-business workloads.

front.text: After applying an aggressive policer on a WAN interface, OSPF neighbors flap. What happened and how do you prevent it?

back.text: The policer is dropping OSPF hellos along with data. Routing protocol traffic uses CS6 (DSCP 48) by default. Always include a class for control plane (match dscp cs6 cs7) and protect it with guaranteed bandwidth or transmit/no-drop action — never police it down indiscriminately.

class-map match-any CONTROL
 match dscp cs6
 match dscp cs7
!
policy-map WAN-OUT
 class CONTROL
  bandwidth percent 5

front.text: Match these DSCP names to their decimal values: EF, AF31, AF41, CS3, CS6, default.
Decode the AF naming scheme: what does AFxy mean?

back.text: x = AF class (1–4), determining queue/bandwidth treatment. y = drop precedence (1–3) within that class — higher y = more likely to drop under congestion. Formula: DSCP = 8x + 2y. Example: AF31 = (8×3) + (2×1) = 26.

front.text: Why must shaping be applied on the subinterface (or with HQoS) for Frame Relay/MPLS subinterface designs?

back.text: Each subinterface represents a separate logical PVC/circuit, often with its own CIR. The physical interface speed isn't the relevant rate. Apply shape to the subinterface (or HQoS parent) at the subscribed rate, then attach a child policy with LLQ/CBWFQ inside that shape.

interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0.100
 encapsulation dot1Q 100
 service-policy output PARENT-SHAPE-20M

front.text: When should match protocol (NBAR) be used vs match dscp?

back.text: Classify deeply once at the edge, mark, then trust DSCP everywhere else.

front.text: What is RSVP and why isn't it used end-to-end in modern enterprise QoS?

back.text: RSVP (Resource Reservation Protocol) is the IntServ signaling protocol that reserves bandwidth per flow along the path. Every router must hold per-flow state, which doesn't scale. Modern designs use DiffServ (per-hop, no state) for scalability. RSVP still appears in MPLS-TE for tunnel signaling.

front.text: What's the difference between auto qos voip cisco-phone, auto qos voip cisco-softphone, and auto qos voip trust?

rows: Conditional trust via CDP — only trusts when phone is detected
Untrusted; classifies by ACLs/DSCP set by softphone app
Unconditional trust on uplink/trunk to other QoS-aware devices

front.text: When you configure bandwidth percent 30 inside a child policy nested under a shape average 20000000, 30% of what?

back.text: 30% of the parent shaper rate (20 Mbps), not the physical interface rate. So this class gets a guaranteed 6 Mbps. This is why HQoS shaping is critical when the contracted rate differs from the link speed.

front.text: What do the three colors green, yellow, and red mean in a three-color policer?

rows: Above Bc but within Be / between CIR and PIR

front.text: On older Catalyst platforms, which command verifies that MLS QoS is enabled globally?

back.text:
show mls qos
show mls qos interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1
show mls qos maps dscp-cos
Catalyst 9000 / IOS-XE platforms use full MQC instead of mls qos commands.

front.text: On a 100 Mbps WAN: 30 voice calls (~3 Mbps), 10 HD video sessions (~40 Mbps), business data, and best-effort. Outline a QoS class structure.

items: VOICE (EF) — priority level 1 percent 5 (5 Mbps)
VIDEO (AF41) — priority level 2 percent 45 (45 Mbps)
SIGNALING (CS3) — bandwidth remaining percent 5
BUSINESS (AF21) — bandwidth remaining percent 50
SCAVENGER (CS1) — bandwidth remaining percent 5
class-default — bandwidth remaining percent 40 + WRED

back.text: Multi-level priority keeps voice safe even when video bursts.

front.text: What does the type qualifier in policy-map type ... do?

back.text: It defines the policy's purpose. Common types: type qos (default — traditional QoS), type queueing (queueing-only on platforms with separate queueing pipeline), type control subscriber (BNG/access), type inspect (firewall). The type determines which class-map types and actions are valid.

front.text: On Catalyst 9000 switches, what is the two-level egress policy model?

back.text: Egress requires a parent type queueing policy that maps DSCP/CoS into ASIC queues, plus an optional non-queueing policy for marking/policing. The queueing policy implements priority/bandwidth/shape; the non-queueing policy handles set/police actions. Both are applied with service-policy output.

front.text: How do you classify all traffic that is NOT DSCP EF?

back.text:
class-map match-all NOT-VOICE
 match not dscp ef
match not inverts a single match condition. Useful for catch-all classes that exclude a specific marking.

front.text: Can class-maps reference other class-maps? Why use this?

back.text: Yes — match class-map nests one class-map inside another. It's used to combine match-any and match-all logic, e.g., to express '(DSCP EF OR CS5) AND in VLAN 10'. The outer class-map enforces match-all over an inner match-any class-map.

class-map match-any RT-DSCP
 match dscp ef
 match dscp cs5
!
class-map match-all VLAN10-RT
 match class-map RT-DSCP
 match vlan 10

front.text: What does queue-limit do inside a policy-map class?

back.text:
policy-map WAN
 class BULK
  bandwidth remaining percent 20
  queue-limit 256 packets

Sets the maximum number of packets buffered in that class before tail drop. Larger queues = more buffering and latency; smaller queues = earlier drops, lower delay. Tune carefully for bulk vs interactive.

front.text: What is bufferbloat?

back.text: Bufferbloat is excessive latency/jitter caused by oversized buffers along a path. Large buffers fill up before any drops occur, so TCP doesn't slow down and packets sit in queues for hundreds of milliseconds. Mitigated with smaller queue limits, AQM (WRED, CoDel), or proper QoS classification.

front.text: Configure DSCP-based WRED in a class so AF13 drops more aggressively than AF11.

back.text:
policy-map WAN
 class DATA
  bandwidth remaining percent 40
  random-detect dscp-based
  random-detect dscp af11 32 40 10
  random-detect dscp af13 16 40 10
Format: random-detect dscp <value> <min-thresh> <max-thresh> <mark-prob-denominator>. AF13's lower min-threshold causes earlier drops than AF11.

front.text: A user remarks all their traffic to EF on their laptop to get priority. The access port trusts DSCP. What's wrong, and how do you fix it?

back.text: Trusting DSCP on an end-user port lets users self-promote traffic. Either disable trust on user ports (re-mark to default at the edge), use conditional trust limited to Cisco IP phones, or apply an ingress policer that re-marks anything above CS5 down to default.

front.text: What is auto qos classify and when is it appropriate?

back.text: auto qos classify treats the port as untrusted and uses NBAR/heuristics to classify traffic and remark it according to Cisco's recommended values. Used on ports facing endpoints that don't mark correctly, like generic PCs or IoT devices. auto qos voip alternatively assumes voice and trusts as configured.

front.text: What can you infer from this output?

Class-map: BUSINESS (match-all)
  Match: dscp af21
  Queueing
   queue limit 64 packets
   (queue depth/total drops/no-buffer drops) 64/45123/0
   (pkts output/bytes output) 1234567/789012345
   bandwidth remaining 50% (4500 kbps)

back.text: BUSINESS class is congested — queue is full (64/64) and 45,123 tail drops have occurred. The class has 50% remaining bandwidth ≈ 4.5 Mbps. Consider increasing queue-limit, enabling WRED, or raising bandwidth allocation.

front.text: How does CoPP differ from data-plane QoS, even though both use MQC?

back.text: CoPP (Control Plane Policing) uses MQC syntax but applies to the control plane rather than an interface. It rate-limits packets punted to the route processor (e.g., BGP, SSH, ICMP) to protect the CPU from DoS. Data-plane QoS prioritizes/drops in the forwarding path.

front.text: Can the LLQ priority queue use more than its configured rate?

back.text: Yes — the implicit policer only engages during congestion. If the link is uncongested, the priority queue can transmit above its configured rate. As soon as congestion appears, the policer drops traffic above the configured priority bandwidth, protecting other classes.

front.text: Show how to apply an output service-policy to a GRE tunnel and ensure underlying physical interface honors it.

back.text:
interface Tunnel1
 ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.0
 tunnel source GigabitEthernet0/0
 tunnel destination 198.51.100.2
 service-policy output WAN-OUT
 qos pre-classify
Without qos pre-classify, classification on the tunnel sees the post-encap header. With it, the policy can match the original inner DSCP/ACL fields.

front.text: Compare per-flow fairness (WFQ/fair-queue) and per-class fairness (CBWFQ).
Why might you set the bandwidth command on an interface even when it doesn't change line speed?

back.text: QoS percent calculations, EIGRP/OSPF metrics, and SNMP utilization graphs all reference the bandwidth value. On a sub-rate WAN handoff (e.g., 50 Mbps logical on a 1 Gbps port), set bandwidth 50000 so QoS percentages and routing metrics reflect reality.

interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0
 bandwidth 50000

front.text: Why does the SP often see more traffic than your shaper suggests it should send?

back.text: By default, IOS shapers measure Layer 3 bytes only. The SP measures including Layer 2 overhead (Ethernet header, preamble, IFG, sometimes ATM cell overhead). Use shape average <rate> account user-defined <overhead> to include L2 framing in the calculation, preventing SP-side policing.

front.text: How does Cisco Catalyst Center simplify QoS deployment?

back.text: Catalyst Center provides an Application Policy workflow: select a site, choose a queueing profile (e.g., 4/8/12-class), and identify business-relevant vs business-irrelevant applications. The controller pushes consistent MQC class-maps, policy-maps, and service-policies to all switches/routers in the site, aligned with RFC 4594.

front.text: show policy-map interface shows the VOICE class with offered rate well below its priority bandwidth, but with steady drops. What likely explains it?

back.text: Microbursts. The 30-second offered rate average masks short bursts that exceed the priority cap during congestion. The implicit priority policer measures over a much shorter window. Either increase the priority bandwidth, raise the burst (priority percent X burst <bytes>), or investigate codec/aggregation behavior causing bursts.

front.text: What is DSCP mutation and where is it useful?

back.text: DSCP mutation is a table-driven remarking that translates incoming DSCP values into different outgoing DSCP values at a domain boundary. Useful between QoS domains with different marking schemes (e.g., enterprise EF=46 vs partner EF=44), without writing a class for each value.

front.text: What are the four classic stages of an enterprise QoS design?

items: Classify and mark at the network edge (closest to the source)
Establish and enforce trust boundaries
Apply queueing, policing, shaping, and congestion avoidance where congestion occurs (typically WAN)
Verify, monitor, and tune using show policy-map, NetFlow, and assurance tools

back.text: Classify once, mark close to the source, trust everywhere else, queue where it congests.

front.text: How do you configure a switch trunk port to trust incoming CoS values?

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet1/0/24
 switchport mode trunk
 auto qos trust cos
Use trust cos when the upstream device is QoS-aware and tags frames properly. Use trust dscp on routed/L3 interfaces or when CoS isn't available.

front.text: Why are routing protocol packets marked CS6 (DSCP 48)?

back.text: CS6 corresponds to the legacy 'Internetwork Control' precedence. It ensures OSPF, EIGRP, BGP, and IS-IS hellos and updates are placed in a high-priority class so adjacencies and routing don't collapse during congestion. CS7 is reserved for very high-priority network control.

front.text: Why does a switch use a CoS-to-DSCP map and what's the default?

back.text: When a switch trusts CoS but later forwards out a routed/L3 interface, it must derive a DSCP from the 3-bit CoS. The default map is CoS x → DSCP 8x (CoS 5 → DSCP 40, not 46). For voice this is wrong, so customize the map: mls qos map cos-dscp 0 8 16 24 32 46 48 56.

front.text: What are the possible QoS trust states for a switch port?

rows: Trust only when expected device (phone) detected

front.text: Configure an egress shaper at 20 Mbps that includes 24 bytes of Layer 2 overhead in its rate calculation.

back.text:
policy-map WAN-PARENT
 class class-default
  shape average 20000000 account user-defined 24
account user-defined adjusts shaping math so the customer router and SP measure the same effective rate.

front.text: What's the difference between match dscp and match precedence in a class-map?

back.text: Use match dscp for modern designs; match precedence remains for legacy interop.

front.text: What is MOS and what range indicates acceptable voice quality?

back.text: MOS (Mean Opinion Score) rates voice quality from 1 (bad) to 5 (excellent). Toll-quality is ~4.0; acceptable enterprise VoIP is 3.5–4.5. MOS is impacted by codec, loss, jitter, and one-way delay — all of which QoS aims to control.

front.text: Approximate per-call bandwidth for G.711 and G.729 over Ethernet (with IP/UDP/RTP headers, 20 ms samples)?
What's the difference between set dscp and set ip dscp in a policy-map?

back.text: set ip dscp marks only IPv4 packets. set dscp (no 'ip') marks both IPv4 and IPv6, and is the modern preferred form. Mixing them can cause IPv6 traffic to remain unmarked if you only used set ip dscp.

front.text: A 1 Gbps switch port dropping output packets even though average utilization is only 200 Mbps. What's likely happening?

back.text: Microbursts — short transient spikes that overflow ASIC buffers. The 5-minute average looks fine, but instantaneous queue depth maxes out. Mitigate with deeper egress buffers, per-queue buffer tuning, or QoS that protects priority traffic during bursts.

front.text: How does the bandwidth statement on an interface affect QoS and EIGRP simultaneously?

back.text: QoS percent allocations are calculated against the bandwidth value. EIGRP uses the same value in its composite metric. So changing bandwidth to model a sub-rate WAN circuit also alters EIGRP path selection — verify routing impact when adjusting it for QoS purposes.

front.text: In an HQoS design, where should the priority class live — parent or child policy?

back.text: In the child policy. The parent shaper sets the aggregate rate; inside the shaped pipe, the child policy schedules among classes with priority/bandwidth statements. Putting priority in the parent doesn't make sense — there's nothing else at that level to compete with.

front.text: What does this snippet imply about the class state?

Class-map: VOICE (match-all)
  Match: dscp ef
  priority 10% (1000 kbps)
  (queue depth/total drops/no-buffer drops) 0/0/0
  (pkts output/bytes output) 1500000/240000000

back.text: Voice class is healthy: queue depth 0 (no backlog), zero drops, traffic flowing. Strict-priority is working correctly and the policer hasn't engaged. No action required.

front.text: What is the default queueing strategy on a Gigabit Ethernet router interface with no service-policy?

back.text: FIFO (first-in, first-out). On high-speed interfaces (>2 Mbps), Cisco IOS defaults to FIFO since congestion is rare. Lower-speed serial interfaces traditionally defaulted to WFQ. Apply MQC service-policy to override.

front.text: Access port has a voice VLAN. Phone marks RTP as DSCP EF and CoS 5. PC behind it marks Skype calls as DSCP EF too. How do you protect the QoS model?

back.text: Use conditional trust on the phone (auto qos voip cisco-phone) so the phone's CDP presence extends trust. The phone is configured to clear CoS/DSCP from frames coming from the attached PC port, so PC self-marking is overwritten before reaching the switch. Phone-originated EF is still honored.

front.text: If a packet matches multiple classes in a policy-map, which class processes it?

back.text: The first matching class in the order they appear in the policy-map. Once matched, subsequent classes are skipped. Order matters — place more specific classes (e.g., VOICE) before general ones (e.g., BUSINESS) and class-default at the end.

front.text: Does IPsec encryption hide DSCP markings from transit devices?

back.text: No. By default, IPsec copies the inner ToS byte to the outer (tunnel-mode) IP header so transit routers can still queue based on DSCP. The payload is encrypted, but the outer DSCP remains visible. ESP transport mode preserves the original IP header (with DSCP) outright.

front.text: Why does Cisco IOS reject a CBWFQ policy where bandwidth percent statements sum to 80%?

back.text: By default, max-reserved-bandwidth is 75%. The remaining 25% is reserved for routing protocols, Layer 2 keepalives, and overhead. Either reduce class allocations to ≤75%, raise max-reserved-bandwidth, or switch to bandwidth remaining percent.

front.text: How do you classify traffic with CoS 5 on a switch trunk port?

back.text:
class-map match-any VOICE-COS
 match cos 5
!
policy-map TRUNK-IN
 class VOICE-COS
  set dscp ef
match cos works only on tagged 802.1Q frames; on untagged access ports use match dscp or match access-group.

front.text: Why is fair-queue often added to class-default in CBWFQ designs?

back.text: Without fair-queue, class-default is a single FIFO, so a few aggressive flows can starve others. Adding fair-queue applies WFQ within the class, giving each flow equitable access to the leftover bandwidth — important because most traffic ends up in class-default.

front.text: What's the difference between show policy-map and show policy-map interface?

rows: Live counters: matches, drops, queue depth, policer/shaper conform/exceed bytes per class

front.text: What is the ITU-T G.114 recommended one-way delay budget for voice?
Why is shaping typically egress-only?

back.text: Shaping buffers excess traffic to smooth bursts. You can only buffer outbound traffic — inbound packets have already been received and consumed link bandwidth. Ingress 'shaping' makes no sense; ingress contention is handled with policing instead.

front.text: Does DSCP survive a routed hop through a Layer 3 device?

back.text: Yes — DSCP lives in the IP header and is preserved across all routed hops by default. Each hop can independently re-mark or honor it via QoS policy, but routing alone does not modify DSCP.

front.text: On Catalyst 9000 series, why might you apply two egress policies — one type queueing and one regular?

back.text: The hardware separates queueing actions (priority, bandwidth, queue-buffers, queue-limit) from non-queueing actions (set, police). The type queueing policy programs ASIC queues, while the non-queueing policy handles marking/policing. Both apply to the same egress interface.

interface GigabitEthernet1/0/24
 service-policy output PARENT-SHAPE
 service-policy type queueing output Q-POLICY

front.text: In a Q-in-Q (802.1ad) environment, how do you classify on the inner customer VLAN?

back.text:
class-map match-all CUSTOMER-VOICE
 match vlan inner 100
match vlan refers to the outer (service provider) tag; match vlan inner refers to the customer-side tag carried inside Q-in-Q encapsulation.

front.text: Compare Catalyst Center QoS deployment vs traditional CLI MQC.
10 Gbps uplink shows tail drops at low average utilization. Application: storage replication and incast TCP. Where do you start?

items: Confirm with show platform hardware fed ... qos queue stats — look for queue depth peaks
Increase egress buffer allocation for the affected queue
Mark storage traffic to a separate class with WRED/ECN
Enable ECN end-to-end so TCP backs off without drops
Consider DCB/PFC if storage is FCoE/RoCE-class traffic

front.text: What is AutoQoS-SRND4 and how does it differ from earlier AutoQoS VoIP?

back.text: AutoQoS-SRND4 generates a richer 8/10-class baseline aligned to Cisco's Smart Routing Network Design v4 — adding classes for video conferencing, streaming, transactional data, scavenger, and more. Earlier auto qos voip only addressed voice/signaling. SRND4 is closer to RFC 4594.

front.text: What is a quick verification checklist after deploying a QoS policy on a WAN router?

items: show run interface — confirm service-policy applied in correct direction
show policy-map interface — verify class matches and counters incrementing
show interfaces — check for total output drops trend
Generate test traffic with known DSCP and confirm it lands in the right class
Compare priority class drops to offered rate; tune burst/bandwidth as needed
Validate routing protocol class isn't dropping (CS6/CS7)

front.text: What four resources does QoS manage, and what does QoS NOT do?

items: Bandwidth
Delay (latency)
Jitter
Packet loss

back.text: QoS does NOT create bandwidth. It only decides what gets priority, what gets delayed, and what gets dropped during congestion.

front.text: Compare real-time and data traffic in a converged network.
What are the four major problems QoS is designed to address?

items: Bandwidth — too many flows competing for limited capacity
Delay (latency) — total time for packet to reach destination
Jitter — variation in delay between packets in same flow
Packet loss — drops due to full queues or errors

front.text: Classify the four delay components as variable or fixed.
What are common causes of jitter, and what symptoms does it produce on voice/video?

items: Causes: congestion, serialization delay, improper fragmentation
Symptoms: choppy voice, unsynchronized video, synthetic-sounding audio

front.text: What is a dejitter buffer and how does it handle early/late packets?

back.text: A dejitter (de-jitter / playout) buffer in IP phones and video endpoints smooths variation in packet arrival times. Packets arriving too fast are buffered for ordered playout; packets arriving too late (after playout deadline) are dropped.

front.text: Compare the three QoS models on guarantees, scalability, and signaling.

back.text: DiffServ + DSCP is the modern, scalable model used in nearly all enterprise QoS designs.

front.text: What are the four major categories of QoS tools?

items: Classification and marking — identify and tag traffic
Policing, shaping, and remarking — control traffic rate
Congestion management — queue and schedule (CBWFQ, LLQ)
Link-specific tools — fragmentation, interleaving, header compression

front.text: List common fields/methods used to classify traffic.

items: CoS (802.1p, 3-bit)
DSCP (6-bit)
IP precedence (3-bit, legacy)
Ingress interface
Source/destination IP or port (ACL)
Application via NBAR/NBAR2

front.text: What is NBAR, and what does NBAR2 improve?

back.text: NBAR (Network Based Application Recognition) performs deep packet inspection at Layers 4–7 to identify applications dynamically for QoS classification and protocol discovery. NBAR2 improves on it with more signatures, better accuracy, cloud-updateable signatures, and greater scalability.

front.text: What is the best-practice rule for where to mark traffic in an enterprise design?

back.text: Classify and mark as close to the traffic source as possible (typically the access edge). Once marked, downstream devices trust the marking and avoid expensive deep classification on every hop.

front.text: Compare the three primary port trust states.

rows: Ignore incoming markings; reset DSCP/CoS to 0
Honor incoming Layer 2 CoS; map to DSCP internally

front.text: What three QoS-relevant fields are inside the 802.1Q tag?
What are the standard meanings of CoS values 0–7?
Why is CoS considered limited compared to DSCP?

back.text: CoS is only 3 bits (8 values) and exists only inside the 802.1Q tag. It is lost on untagged ports, across non-Ethernet links, and on routed (Layer 3) hops. DSCP (6 bits, 64 values) lives in the IP header and survives every routed hop.

front.text: What are the four WMM access categories and their Cisco WLC profile names?
Compare IP precedence and DSCP.
What is ECN and what does it require to function?

back.text: ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification) signals congestion to endpoints by marking packets instead of dropping them. It requires ECN-capable endpoints (TCP stacks) and WRED-capable routers along the path. ECN reduces retransmissions and improves throughput under congestion.

front.text: Summarize the four DSCP per-hop behaviors (PHBs).

rows: Assured Forwarding — x = class, y = drop precedence
Class Selector — backward-compatible with IP precedence

front.text: What is the default CoS-to-DSCP mapping table?

back.text: CoS 5 maps to DSCP 40 (CS5) by default — NOT 46 (EF). For VoIP, customize the map or trust DSCP directly.

front.text: Compare policing and shaping on drops, buffering, delay, and direction.
Define CIR and PIR.

rows: Committed Information Rate — guaranteed contracted rate
Peak Information Rate — maximum burstable rate above CIR

front.text: What is the main risk of pure strict priority queuing, and how is it mitigated in LLQ?

back.text: Pure strict priority can starve lower-priority queues if priority traffic is unbounded. LLQ mitigates this with a built-in implicit policer on the priority queue that enforces the configured rate during congestion, protecting CBWFQ classes.

front.text: Compare CBWFQ and LLQ on bandwidth, latency, and strict priority.

cards.subtopic: Tail drop and TCP global sync

front.text: What is TCP global synchronization and how does WRED help?

back.text: Tail drop discards all packets once a queue is full. Many TCP flows hit the wall at the same instant, all back off, then ramp up together — causing oscillating link utilization (global synchronization). WRED randomly drops packets earlier (before full), spreading TCP backoffs and keeping utilization steady.

front.text: Compare RED and WRED.
List the three MQC deployment steps in order.

items: Define class-map(s) — classify traffic (e.g., VOICE, WEB)
Define policy-map — apply actions (bandwidth, priority, police, shape)
Apply with service-policy input|output to an interface

front.text: Match each MQC keyword to its function: bandwidth, priority, police, shape.

rows: Strict-priority LLQ queue with implicit policer
Drop or remark traffic exceeding rate
Buffer/smooth traffic to a defined rate

front.text: How does Catalyst Center (DNA Center) deploy QoS using intent-based concepts?

items: Application sets — groups of apps managed together
Business relevance groups — Business Relevant, Default, Business Irrelevant
Site scope — policy applied per site/area
Controller converts the intent into device CLI MQC config aligned with RFC 4594

front.text: List standard markings for Voice RTP, Video, Network Control, and Scavenger.

back.text: Memorize EF=46, CS6=48, CS1=8, AF41=34 — these appear repeatedly on the exam.

front.text: True or false: QoS can create additional bandwidth on a congested link.

back.text: False. QoS cannot create bandwidth. It only decides which traffic gets priority, which is delayed, and which is dropped. The only real fix for sustained congestion is more bandwidth — QoS protects critical traffic in the meantime.

front.text: Beyond tail drop, what other drop counters appear on Cisco interfaces?

items: Input queue drops — ingress queue full
Ignore — interface couldn't read packet (buffer issue)
Overrun — receiver overrun, hardware can't keep up
Frame errors — CRC, runts, giants
Output drops — egress congestion (most QoS-relevant)

front.text: What factors influence processing delay on a router?

items: CPU performance and load
Switching method (CEF vs process switching)
Enabled features (NAT, encryption, ACLs, NetFlow)
Packet inspection depth (NBAR, firewall)

front.text: Which factors determine queuing delay?

items: Queue depth at time of arrival
Packet sizes ahead in the queue
Egress interface speed
Queuing/scheduling algorithm in use (FIFO, WFQ, CBWFQ, LLQ)

front.text: What is the relationship between link speed and serialization delay?

back.text: Serialization delay is inversely proportional to link speed: lower bandwidth means each bit takes longer to clock onto the wire. That is why slow WAN links (<768 kbps) need LFI to keep voice serialization delay low.

front.text: What two physical factors set propagation delay?

back.text: Distance and media type. Propagation delay is the time for the signal to physically travel from sender to receiver — fiber/copper transmits at ~2/3 the speed of light. It is fixed and cannot be reduced by QoS.

front.text: Describe the Best-Effort QoS model and where it is the default.

back.text: Best-Effort applies no prioritization — all packets are treated equally and forwarded FIFO. It is simple and highly scalable, with no guarantees on delay, loss, or jitter. It is the default IP behavior unless QoS is configured.

front.text: What are the defining characteristics of the IntServ model?

items: Per-flow resource reservation via RSVP signaling
End-to-end guarantees on bandwidth and delay/loss
Each router maintains per-flow state
Poor scalability — rarely used end-to-end today
Still relevant in MPLS-TE for tunnel signaling

front.text: What are the defining characteristics of the DiffServ model?

items: Hop-by-hop QoS — each device acts independently
Uses DSCP markings to identify class
Class-based treatment (Voice, Video, Best Effort, Scavenger)
Highly scalable — no per-flow state
Most common modern enterprise QoS model

front.text: What are the link-specific QoS tools and where are they used?

items: Link Fragmentation and Interleaving (LFI) — fragments large packets so voice can interleave on slow links
Header compression (cRTP, TCP header compression) — reduces per-packet overhead on slow links
Both are typically applied on WAN links below ~768 kbps

front.text: What happens to QoS markings on a port in the untrusted state?

back.text: Incoming CoS and DSCP values are ignored and rewritten to 0 (Best Effort). This is the safe default for user-facing access ports, ensuring users cannot self-promote their traffic into priority classes.

front.text: What is the DEI bit in an 802.1Q tag?

back.text: DEI (Drop Eligible Indicator) is a 1-bit field replacing the legacy CFI bit. When set, the frame is marked as preferentially droppable during congestion — analogous to yellow color in a three-color policer at Layer 2.

front.text: What is User Priority (UP) in 802.11e/WMM, and how does it relate to access categories?

back.text: UP is a 3-bit value (0–7) carried in the 802.11e QoS Control field, similar to CoS. Each UP value maps into one of four Access Categories (AC_BK, AC_BE, AC_VI, AC_VO), which determine contention parameters (CWmin, CWmax, AIFS) — voice gets the most aggressive contention behavior, background the least.

front.text: Where exactly is DSCP located in the IPv4 header?

back.text: DSCP occupies the top 6 bits of the 8-bit ToS (DS) byte in the IPv4 header (byte 1, just after Version/IHL). The remaining 2 bits are ECN. In IPv6 the same 8-bit field is called Traffic Class.

front.text: What are the four ECN 2-bit code points and their meanings?
Order AF31, AF32, and AF33 from least to most likely to be dropped under congestion.

items: AF31 — lowest drop precedence (least likely to be dropped)
AF32 — medium drop precedence
AF33 — highest drop precedence (most likely to be dropped)

back.text: All three share the same AF class 3 queue/bandwidth — only the second digit (drop precedence) changes.

front.text: Why is voice marked EF (46) instead of CS5 (40), even though they map from the same CoS 5?

back.text: EF is a dedicated DiffServ PHB explicitly defined for low-latency, low-loss strict-priority forwarding. CS5 is the legacy class-selector mapping for IP precedence 5 with no specific PHB guarantees. EF is the modern recommended marking for VoIP RTP.

front.text: When does network congestion occur?

back.text: When the offered ingress traffic rate exceeds the available egress capacity of an interface. Packets begin queuing, queues grow, and eventually drops occur. QoS only engages meaningfully during congestion.

front.text: Compare Round Robin and Weighted Fair Queuing scheduling.
What scheduling does CBWFQ use, and what does it guarantee vs not guarantee?

back.text: CBWFQ uses Weighted Round Robin (WRR) scheduling across user-defined classes. It guarantees minimum bandwidth per class during congestion, but does NOT guarantee latency — that is why voice/video need LLQ instead of plain CBWFQ.

front.text: What is LLQ in one sentence?

back.text: LLQ (Low Latency Queuing) is CBWFQ plus a strict-priority queue with an implicit policer — providing both bandwidth and latency guarantees, ideal for voice and real-time video.

front.text: Define tail drop and its main side effect on TCP.

back.text: Tail drop discards arriving packets only once the queue is completely full. With many TCP flows, this triggers global synchronization — flows back off and ramp up together, causing oscillating utilization and poor throughput.

front.text: What is RED and what problem does it solve?

back.text: RED (Random Early Detection) randomly drops packets before the queue is full. By dropping selectively and earlier, RED throttles individual TCP senders at different times, preventing TCP global synchronization and keeping utilization stable.

front.text: How does WRED differentiate between more and less important traffic?

back.text: WRED assigns lower drop probabilities to higher-priority DSCP/IP precedence values and higher drop probabilities to lower-priority values. So scavenger/AFx3 traffic is dropped earlier than business/AFx1 — protecting more important classes during congestion.

front.text: Show a minimal MQC class-map for voice traffic and a CBWFQ class for web traffic.

back.text:
class-map match-any VOICE
 match dscp ef
!
class-map match-any WEB
 match protocol http

front.text: Show a policy-map that uses priority for voice, bandwidth for web, and apply it outbound.

back.text:
policy-map WAN-OUT
 class VOICE
  priority percent 10
 class WEB
  bandwidth percent 30
 class class-default
  fair-queue
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0
 service-policy output WAN-OUT

front.text: What is the main purpose and benefit of Cisco AutoQoS?

items: Automated QoS deployment with a single command
Faster, consistent rollout aligned to Cisco-validated templates
Reduces complexity for VoIP and enterprise traffic baselines
Output is regular MQC config that can be customized after generation

front.text: What three business-relevance categories does Cisco Catalyst Center / DNA Center use to classify applications?

items: Business Relevant — apps critical to the business (mapped to higher-priority classes)
Default — apps neither critical nor irrelevant (Best Effort)
Business Irrelevant — non-business apps (mapped to scavenger/CS1)

front.text: Why does DiffServ scale far better than IntServ?

back.text: DiffServ acts per-hop based on a 6-bit DSCP marking with no per-flow state — every router treats marked packets the same way. IntServ requires per-flow reservation state on every router along the path, which does not scale to thousands of concurrent flows.

front.text: Although policing is listed as 'ingress-focused,' where else can it be applied and why?

back.text: Policing can also run on egress. Egress policing is used to enforce SLAs/contracts going to a service provider, or to rate-limit a class within a parent shaper. Ingress policing is preferred when you want to drop unwanted/scavenger traffic early before it consumes backplane resources.

front.text: When does QoS actually have any effect on traffic?

back.text: Only during congestion. If an interface is uncongested, all packets are forwarded immediately and QoS scheduling/dropping never engages. Marking and classification still happen, but priority/bandwidth/WRED only matter when there is contention for the link.

front.text: What are the standard one-way targets for VoIP delay, jitter, and loss?
In one line each, contrast real-time and data traffic for QoS purposes.

items: Real-time — low and predictable bandwidth, very sensitive to delay/jitter/loss
Data — bursty, bandwidth-hungry, mostly tolerant of delay and loss (TCP recovers)

front.text: Why is the default switch CoS-to-DSCP map insufficient for VoIP?

back.text: By default CoS 5 maps to DSCP 40 (CS5), not DSCP 46 (EF). Voice should ride on EF for proper LLQ treatment downstream. Either customize the CoS-to-DSCP map (e.g., mls qos map cos-dscp 0 8 16 24 32 46 48 56) or trust DSCP directly on the ingress port.

front.text: How many DSCP code points exist and why is that important?

back.text: DSCP is 6 bits, so 64 values (0–63). This gives DiffServ much finer granularity than the legacy 3-bit IP precedence (8 values) or 802.1p CoS (8 values), enabling rich traffic class hierarchies like the 8/12-class enterprise models.

front.text: What is the BE PHB and what DSCP value does it use?

back.text: BE (Best Effort, also called Default Forwarding / DF) uses DSCP 0. It is the default treatment for unclassified or unmarked traffic — no special bandwidth, latency, or drop guarantees.

front.text: What kind of traffic is EF designed to carry, and why?

back.text: EF (Expedited Forwarding, DSCP 46) is designed for traffic requiring low loss, low latency, low jitter, and assured bandwidth — primarily VoIP RTP. It is normally placed in an LLQ strict-priority queue at every hop.

front.text: Decode the AFxy naming format.

rows: Drop precedence within the class — higher y = dropped first

back.text: Formula: DSCP = 8x + 2y. Example: AF41 = 8(4)+2(1) = 34.

front.text: What are the DSCP decimal values for CS1, CS3, CS5, CS6, and CS7?
Why is marking near the source/edge a best practice?

back.text: Marking once at the edge eliminates the need for repeated deep packet inspection at every hop. Distribution, core, and WAN devices can simply trust DSCP and act on it, which is faster (often hardware-accelerated) and more scalable.

front.text: What is the trust boundary?

back.text: The trust boundary is the point in the network where QoS markings from external devices are accepted as valid. Inside the boundary, devices honor markings; outside, they re-mark or zero them. It should be placed as close to the source as possible — ideally at the access switch using conditional trust to a Cisco IP phone.

front.text: Why is DiffServ described as 'hop-by-hop' QoS?

back.text: Each device along the path independently inspects the DSCP marking and applies its locally configured per-hop behavior — without negotiating or reserving anything end-to-end. Consistent QoS is achieved by configuring matching DiffServ policies on each hop.

front.text: What does RSVP signal, and what state does it create on routers?

back.text: RSVP signals a per-flow reservation request along the path with desired bandwidth and delay/loss characteristics. Each router on the path stores per-flow reservation state and admits or rejects the request. This is what makes IntServ powerful but unscalable.

front.text: Why is DSCP considered persistent across networks?

back.text: DSCP is in the IP header, which is preserved across all routed hops and Layer 3 boundaries. Unless a device explicitly re-marks it, DSCP travels end-to-end — across LANs, WANs, and the Internet — making it ideal for end-to-end DiffServ designs.

front.text: Why is data traffic described as bursty, and how does QoS handle that?

back.text: TCP-based applications send in bursts based on window size and ACK clocking — long idle periods followed by short high-rate sends. QoS handles this with shaping (smooths bursts), CBWFQ (guarantees minimum bandwidth), and WRED (drops earlier to avoid TCP global synchronization).

front.text: What event triggers tail drop on an interface?

back.text: Packets arriving when the egress (output) queue is already full. Without congestion avoidance like WRED, the new packet is simply dropped — regardless of its priority — until the queue drains.

front.text: What trade-off does a larger dejitter buffer introduce?

back.text: A larger dejitter buffer absorbs more jitter and drops fewer late packets, but increases end-to-end delay because packets sit longer before playout. The buffer size must balance jitter tolerance against the 150 ms one-way delay budget for voice.

front.text: Without any QoS configured, how does the network treat all packets?

back.text: All packets receive Best Effort treatment — equally forwarded in FIFO order on each interface. There are no guarantees on delay, jitter, loss, or bandwidth. This is the default IP behavior.

front.text: Why does IntServ scale poorly in large networks?

back.text: Every router along the path must maintain per-flow reservation state and process RSVP refresh messages. With thousands of concurrent flows, control-plane CPU and memory consumption become unmanageable. DiffServ avoids this by acting per-packet based on DSCP only.

front.text: Name the four traffic class buckets typically used in a basic DiffServ design.

items: Voice
Video
Best Effort
Scavenger

back.text: Enterprise designs commonly extend this to 8 or 12 classes per RFC 4594 (e.g., adding signaling, network control, transactional, bulk).

front.text: What two requirements must be in place for ECN to actually replace drops?

items: Endpoints must be ECN-capable (TCP stack negotiates ECN at SYN)
Routers must support and have WRED with ECN enabled to mark CE instead of dropping

front.text: What command attaches an MQC policy-map to an interface, and in which directions can it be applied?

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0
 service-policy input EDGE-IN
 service-policy output WAN-OUT
input — for ingress policies (classify, mark, police). output — for egress policies (queueing, shaping, WRED, policing).

front.text: What does the bandwidth keyword inside a policy-map do?

back.text: It provides a CBWFQ minimum bandwidth guarantee for the class during congestion. The class can use more bandwidth when others are idle, but during contention it is guaranteed at least the configured value (in kbps, percent, or remaining percent).

front.text: What does the priority keyword inside a policy-map create?

back.text: An LLQ strict-priority queue with an implicit policer. Traffic in this class is serviced first (low latency, low jitter), but is policed at the configured rate during congestion to prevent starving other classes.

front.text: What does the police keyword inside a policy-map do?

back.text: It enforces a rate limit on the class by dropping or remarking traffic that exceeds the configured CIR (and optionally PIR). Police does NOT buffer — excess traffic is acted on immediately.

front.text: What does the shape keyword inside a policy-map do?

back.text: It buffers excess traffic and releases it at the configured rate, smoothing bursts to match a contracted CIR. Used on egress (typically WAN handoffs) to avoid SP-side policing.

front.text: True or false: setting DSCP EF on a packet automatically gives it priority forwarding.

back.text: False. Marking by itself only adds a label. Priority forwarding requires an egress policy on each congested interface that classifies on EF and places it into a priority/LLQ queue. Without that policy, EF traffic is treated like Best Effort during congestion.

cards.subtopic: Cisco DNA / Catalyst Center site scope

front.text: What is 'site scope' in the Catalyst Center QoS workflow?

back.text: Site scope defines the area (global, area, site, building, floor) over which an application policy applies. Catalyst Center pushes consistent class-maps and policy-maps to all in-scope devices, ensuring uniform QoS treatment across the site without per-device manual configuration.

front.text: What does 'intent-based' mean in the context of Catalyst Center QoS?

back.text: Administrators describe the desired outcome — which apps are business-relevant, default, or irrelevant — and the controller translates that intent into appropriate device-level CLI (class-maps, policy-maps, service-policies) aligned with Cisco-validated and RFC 4594-based templates.

front.text: Provide a final quick-reference: which QoS tool category fixes which problem?

rows: Excessive bandwidth use by a class

front.text: What is the fundamental purpose of QoS as defined in the ENCOR exam topics?

back.text: QoS is the ability of a network to predictably provide business applications with the service required for them to be successfully used. Its fundamental purpose is to manage contention for network resources to maximize the end-user experience of a session.

front.text: What four service characteristics does QoS aim to deliver to business applications?

items: Dedicated bandwidth (guaranteed minimum)
Controlled jitter (low delay variation)
Controlled latency (low and predictable delay)
Improved loss characteristics (managed packet drops)

cards.subtopic: QoS not a substitute for bandwidth

front.text: What is the key warning about QoS and bandwidth shortage?

back.text: QoS is NOT a substitute for bandwidth. If a network is congested, packets will be dropped — QoS only controls how, when, and what traffic is dropped. The real solution to sustained congestion is more bandwidth; QoS protects critical traffic in the meantime.

front.text: When there is contention on a link, what does QoS decide?

items: Which sessions get priority (bypass other queues)
Which sessions get delayed (queued behind priority)
Which sessions get dropped (less important or recoverable)

front.text: Why do converged networks make QoS necessary?

back.text: Voice, video, and data — once carried on dedicated networks — now share infrastructure. Voice/video are intolerant of delay, jitter, and loss; data is bursty and bandwidth-hungry. Without QoS to mediate, voice and video quality is severely compromised during congestion.

front.text: What user-visible symptoms occur on a converged network without QoS?

items: Voice: choppy, unintelligible, or echo/talker overlap
Video: jerky images, audio out of sync with video
Calls fail to connect (dropped signaling packets)
Interactive apps appear unresponsive (virtual desktop, brokerage, call centers)

front.text: Define end-to-end delay (network latency).

back.text: End-to-end delay is the time it takes for a packet to reach the receiving endpoint after the sender transmits it. It is the sum of all variable-delay components (processing + queuing) and fixed-delay components (serialization + propagation) along the path.

front.text: How do variable and fixed delay components differ as packet size and conditions change?

back.text: Variable-delay components (processing, queuing) can change based on network conditions even for packets of the same size. Fixed-delay components (serialization, propagation) increase linearly with packet size but remain constant for packets of the same size.

front.text: What are the three primary ways to manage delay on a link?

items: Upgrade link bandwidth (reduces serialization and queuing delay)
Use a queuing technique to prioritize critical traffic
Enable header/payload compression to reduce bits transmitted

front.text: On a PPP link, what is jitter almost always caused by, and what mitigates it?

back.text: On PPP encapsulation, jitter is almost always due to serialization delay. It is mitigated with Link Fragmentation and Interleaving (LFI), which fragments large packets so voice can interleave between fragments, reducing serialization delay.

front.text: How can fragmentation itself become a cause of jitter?

back.text: If LFI is incorrectly configured on a slow-speed link, media (voice/video) packets may themselves be fragmented. Reassembling fragments adds variable delay, which increases jitter — the opposite of LFI's intended effect. Tune fragment size so media packets pass through whole.

front.text: Compare 'choppy' voice and 'synthetic-sounding' voice as jitter symptoms.

rows: Gaps where syllables are dropped/badly delayed in start-stop fashion
Predictive insertion replaces lost samples with a best guess from a previous sample, creating quiver/fuzziness

front.text: Describe how a dejitter (playout) buffer behaves at call start and during operation.

back.text: When a call starts, the dejitter buffer fills to a baseline depth.
