During the call: packets arriving too fast are buffered (queue grows); packets arriving too slowly cause the queue to empty; packets arriving beyond the buffer's holding capacity are dropped immediately, causing audible dropouts.

front.text: What is the most common cause of packet loss on a router, and what is it called?

back.text: Routers run out of space in a particular interface's output queue. New arriving packets are dropped at the tail of the queue — this is called tail drop (or output drop).

front.text: Match each less-common drop cause to its meaning: input queue drop, ignore, overrun, frame errors.

rows: Main CPU congested; cannot process packets
Router ran out of buffer space
CPU congested; cannot assign a free buffer to the new packet
Hardware-detected error: CRC, runt, or giant

front.text: What three approaches manage packet loss due to tail drop?

items: Increase the link bandwidth
Use a queuing technique that guarantees bandwidth and buffer space for loss-sensitive apps
Prevent congestion by shaping or selectively dropping (WRED) before congestion occurs

front.text: Which postal-style analogy describes the Best-Effort QoS model?

back.text: Standard postal mail. Your letter is treated the same as every other letter — it may never arrive, and unless you have a separate notification arrangement, you may never know. No prioritization, no guarantees, no signaling — but simple and infinitely scalable.

front.text: Which delivery analogy describes the IntServ QoS model?

back.text: A private courier airplane or truck dedicated to your traffic. It guarantees quality and delivery (bandwidth, delay, loss), but is expensive and unscalable because resources are reserved per flow and not shared.

front.text: Which delivery analogy describes the DiffServ QoS model?

back.text: A package delivery service where you choose (and pay for) a service level when you ship. Throughout the network, the service level is recognized and your package gets preferential or normal treatment based on that class. Scalable, hop-by-hop, and class-based.

front.text: What does RSVP install along the path when an IntServ reservation is accepted?

back.text: A traffic classifier in the QoS forwarding path on each router. The classifier tells the QoS forwarding logic how to identify packets of that flow and which forwarding treatment (bandwidth, delay) to apply. Each router maintains state for that individual flow.

front.text: Why is DiffServ NOT considered an end-to-end QoS strategy?

back.text: DiffServ enforces QoS independently on each device (hop-by-hop) without signaling between hops. End-to-end guarantees cannot be enforced — they depend on consistent configuration at every hop. The advantage is scalability: thousands of apps map to a small set of classes.

front.text: Why should classification happen as few times as possible in a network?

back.text: Deep classification (especially Layer 4–7 inspection) consumes time, CPU, and memory. Classify once at the ingress edge, mark the packet, and let downstream devices simply trust and act on the marking — much faster and often hardware-accelerated.

front.text: When is reclassification and re-marking common in practice?

back.text: At hand-off points between networks — for example, where the enterprise meets a service provider, or between QoS domains with different marking schemes. The receiving network may have its own trust policy and may not honor markings from outside.

front.text: List the common traffic descriptors used to classify packets per the ENCOR cram.

items: Class of service (CoS)
Incoming interface
IP precedence
DSCP
Source or destination address
Application (via NBAR/NBAR2)
MPLS EXP bits

front.text: Compare NBAR active mode and NBAR passive (Protocol Discovery) mode.

rows: Used inside MQC class-maps (match protocol …) to classify traffic for QoS
Enabled per-interface to discover applications and provide real-time per-protocol statistics

front.text: Name several Cisco platforms that support NBAR2.

items: ISR Generation 2
ISR 4400 series
Cisco ASR 1000
Cisco CSR 1000V (Cloud Services Router)
Cisco ASA-CX (Context-Aware Security)
Cisco wireless LAN controllers (WLCs)

front.text: How is NBAR/NBAR2 protocol/signature support updated without an IOS upgrade?

back.text: By installing a Packet Description Language Module (PDLM) on legacy NBAR systems, or a Protocol Pack on NBAR2 systems. Both allow nondisruptive updates so you can recognize new applications without changing the base IOS image.

front.text: Show how to match an NBAR2 attribute category in a class-map.

back.text:
class-map match-any EMAIL-CAT
 match protocol attribute category email
!
class-map match-any GAMING-CAT
 match protocol attribute category gaming

Categories include browsing, business-and-productivity-tools, email, file-sharing, gaming, instant-messaging, internet-security, and many more — useful for broad classification without naming every protocol.

front.text: Why does marking save CPU and memory on downstream devices?

back.text: Once marked, downstream devices only need to look at a few bits in the header (CoS or DSCP) instead of inspecting deep into packet payloads to identify applications. This is far cheaper and is often offloaded to hardware.

front.text: In the standard QoS trust boundary diagram, what do lines 1, 2, and 3 represent?

rows: Extended to a trusted endpoint (e.g., Cisco IP phone)
Stops at the access switch (untrusted endpoint)
Pushed back to the distribution layer

front.text: What are the three static trust states a Catalyst switch port can hold?

rows: Discard L2/L3 markings; generate internal DSCP 0
Accept CoS marking; calculate internal DSCP via CoS-to-DSCP map
Accept DSCP marking directly as internal DSCP

front.text: How does dynamic (conditional) trust work, and which protocol enables it?

back.text: Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP) identifies the connected endpoint. When the switch detects a Cisco endpoint that should be trusted (such as a Cisco IP phone), the trust boundary is automatically extended to that device. If CDP is lost or the device changes, trust is automatically removed. It only works for Cisco end devices.

front.text: What two 2-byte fields does the 802.1Q standard insert into an Ethernet frame, and where?

rows: Contains PCP (3 bits), DEI (1 bit), VLAN ID (12 bits)

back.text: TPID + TCI together = 4-byte 802.1Q tag inserted after the source MAC address.

front.text: Why does 802.1p CoS value 1 effectively communicate a LOWER priority than CoS 0?

back.text: The default priority for end-station transmission is 0 (Best Effort). Changing that default would break interoperability. So CoS 0 doubles as both 'default priority' AND 'Best Effort'. CoS 1 is then assigned to Background — which is below Best Effort. As a result, CoS 1 < CoS 0 in priority.

front.text: Under what conditions are CoS markings lost?

back.text: Frames lose their CoS markings when transiting any non-802.1Q or non-802.1p link — including any non-Ethernet WAN link, untagged access ports, or routed (Layer 3) hops where the 802.1Q tag is stripped. For network transit, use Layer 3 DSCP marking instead.

front.text: Why is the wireless access point the second-most-likely congestion point in an enterprise (after WAN), and why is QoS important on the WLAN?

back.text: Wireless media is typically slower, half-duplex, and shared. QoS minimizes packet drops due to congestion and reduces jitter caused by nondeterministic access to the half-duplex shared medium — both critical for voice/video over Wi-Fi.

front.text: Show how 802.11e User Priority values pair into the four Access Categories.
How does Cisco wireless reclassify voice and video CoS values vs the IEEE 802.11 default, and why?

back.text: Cisco follows IETF RFC 4594, mapping voice to CoS 5 and video to CoS 4 (instead of IEEE's 6 and 5). This frees CoS 6 and 7 for true Layer 3 network control. The Cisco Unified Wireless Network performs conversions at the wireless-wired boundary to remain compliant with both standards.

front.text: Where does Layer 3 marking occur in IPv6 packets vs IPv4?

back.text: In IPv4, marking lives in the ToS byte. In IPv6, the equivalent field is the Traffic Class byte. Both are 8 bits and use the same DSCP (6 bits) + ECN (2 bits) layout, so the same DSCP values apply identically to IPv4 and IPv6.

front.text: How many usable classes does IP Precedence offer for user traffic?

back.text: Six. IP Precedence is 3 bits (values 0–7), but values 6 and 7 are reserved for internal network use (routing, control). That leaves precedence 0–5 = six usable classes for user traffic.

front.text: DSCP defines 64 possible values, but how many are commonly used in practice?

back.text: About 32. DSCP encodes 0–63, but enterprise designs commonly use a defined subset: BE (0), the AFxy values, the CSx values, and EF (46). The remaining values exist but are rarely deployed.

front.text: Why does Cisco IOS require WRED for ECN marking to function?

back.text: ECN marking depends on active queue management — the router must detect impending congestion (queue filling) before the queue is full. WRED provides exactly that mechanism by probabilistically choosing packets to act on. So Cisco performs ECN marking only when WRED is configured on the queue.

front.text: Which RFCs define the BE, AF, and EF per-hop behaviors?

rows: RFC 2474 / RFC 2475 (with RFC 2547 referenced in source)

front.text: What is the simple rule for mapping a CoS value to its default Class Selector / DSCP value?

back.text: Take the CoS 3-bit value as the top 3 bits of a 6-bit DSCP and append three zeros. Numerically: DSCP = CoS × 8. Examples: CoS 5 → DSCP 40 (CS5), CoS 6 → DSCP 48 (CS6), CoS 3 → DSCP 24 (CS3).

front.text: When traffic exceeds a policer's allowed rate, what two actions can the policer take?

items: Drop the excess traffic
Re-mark the excess traffic to a different class — usually one with a higher drop probability (e.g., remark AF11 → AF13 or to scavenger CS1)

cards.subtopic: Why shape on egress to SP

front.text: Why are shapers commonly deployed on enterprise-to-SP egress links?

back.text: To ensure traffic going to the service provider does not exceed the contracted CIR. If it did, the SP would police it and likely drop the excess. Shaping on the customer side smooths bursts to fit within the contracted rate, avoiding SP-side drops.

front.text: How do policing and shaping differ in their impact on TCP behavior, delay, and jitter?
What is the difference between queuing and scheduling?

rows: Buffers packets and reorders them so higher-priority traffic exits sooner
Decides which packet should be sent out next

front.text: Define traffic starvation in the context of strict priority queuing.

back.text: Traffic starvation occurs when a strict-priority queue is always being serviced, leaving lower-priority queues unserviced indefinitely. Lower-priority traffic is effectively never delivered as long as priority traffic is present. LLQ mitigates this with an implicit policer on the priority queue.

front.text: Match each older queuing method to its key characteristic: FIFO, PQ, CQ, WFQ.

rows: Single queue, packets sent in arrival order
4 queues in strict priority order — risks starvation
16 queues in round-robin — no strict priority for real-time
Bandwidth divided among flows fairly — no per-flow guarantees

front.text: What is the stated objective of QoS technologies in a converged network?

back.text: To make voice, video, and data convergence appear transparent to end users by allowing different traffic types to contend inequitably for network resources, granting priority/preferential service to voice, video, and critical applications so their quality is not degraded.

front.text: What five specific features does QoS provide?

items: Bandwidth guarantees
Buffering capabilities and dropping mechanisms
Traffic policing
Traffic priorities
Network traffic shaping

front.text: Per the ENCOR Exam Alert: which four factors determine the transmission quality of the network?

items: Packet loss
Delay
Jitter
Lack of bandwidth

back.text: Exam Alert: Know how packet loss, delay, jitter, and lack of bandwidth affect the transmission quality of the network.

front.text: Why is service availability described as an essential foundation for QoS?

back.text: QoS only matters when the network is up and forwarding. A highly available infrastructure is the prerequisite — packet loss during non-congestion periods should be essentially zero, leaving QoS to manage drops only during congestion. Without availability, no amount of QoS can deliver predictable service.

front.text: Define packet loss in QoS terms.

back.text: Packet loss is a relative measure of the number of packets that were not received compared to the total number of packets initially transmitted. It is generally a function of availability — design networks to be highly available so non-congestion loss is essentially zero.

front.text: What four approaches address packet loss?

items: Increase the speed of the link
Implement QoS congestion-avoidance and congestion-management techniques
Implement traffic policing to drop low-priority packets and let high-priority traffic across
Implement traffic shaping to delay packets instead of dropping them when bursts exceed buffer capacity

front.text: Exam Focus: which delay types are fixed and which is variable?

back.text:
Propagation | Fixed
Serialization | Fixed
Processing | Fixed (but practically variable based on CPU)
Jitter (delay variation) | Variable

The groups propagation, serialization, and processing under fixed delay, with jitter as the variable component.

front.text: Define propagation delay.

back.text: Propagation delay is the time it takes for a packet to travel from a source endpoint to a destination endpoint over a fiber-optic or copper medium. It depends on physical distance and media type and cannot be reduced by QoS.

front.text: Define serialization delay and state its relationship to link speed.

back.text: Serialization delay is the time it takes to put all the bits of a packet onto a link. It is fixed and depends on link speed: the higher the speed of the link, the lower the serialization delay.

front.text: What does processing delay account for?

back.text: Processing delay is the time it takes for a router or switch to take a packet from an input interface and place the packet into the output queue of the output interface.

front.text: Which four factors influence processing delay?

items: Packet switching mode (process switching, software CEF, or hardware CEF)
Router architecture (centralized or distributed)
CPU load
CPU speed on software-based platforms

front.text: If one packet takes 100 ms end-to-end and the next takes 125 ms, what is the jitter?

back.text: 25 ms. Jitter (delay variation) is the difference in end-to-end delay between consecutive packets — here, 125 ms − 100 ms = 25 ms.

front.text: What is the rule about end-to-end bandwidth on a packet data path?

back.text: The available end-to-end bandwidth equals the lowest-bandwidth link's capacity in the path. Once that link is exceeded, congestion and drops occur. The simple fix is more bandwidth on that link, but where that's not feasible, QoS (policing/queuing) prioritizes critical traffic over the bottleneck.

front.text: What three factors should you consider when deciding which QoS service model to deploy?

items: Know the application or problem you are trying to solve — each model fits certain applications
Know what kind of capability you want to allocate to your resources
Understand the cost-benefit analysis — DiffServ deployment costs more than Best Effort

front.text: How does an application behave under the Best-Effort QoS model?

back.text: The application sends data whenever it needs, in any quantity, without requesting permission or informing the network. Packets are delivered without assurance of reliability, delay bounds, or throughput. Suitable for general file transfers and email.

front.text: Describe the IntServ workflow for an application that needs guaranteed service.

items: Application explicitly signals the network with its traffic profile and required QoS
Network admits or rejects the request and reserves resources end-to-end
On confirmation, the application sends data within the described traffic profile
Network maintains per-flow state; each router classifies, polices, and queues based on that state

front.text: What two service types does IntServ (with intelligent queuing + RSVP) provide?

rows: Applications can reserve bandwidth to meet their requirements
Applications can have low delay and high throughput even during network congestion

front.text: Which QoS implementation model is most commonly used?

back.text: DiffServ. It is the most commonly used QoS implementation model — used for mission-critical applications and providing end-to-end QoS without per-flow signaling, instead relying on per-packet DSCP/CoS classification at each hop.

front.text: How does an application or device 'specify' QoS treatment in DiffServ without signaling?

back.text: By marking fields like IP precedence bits, DSCP, or via source/destination addresses. Each router uses that specification to classify, mark, shape, police, and queue traffic — without explicit RSVP-style signaling between hops.

front.text: Exam Focus: Which Cisco IOS features support the DiffServ model?

items: Committed Access Rate (CAR) — metering and policing for bandwidth management
Intelligent queuing schemes — WRED (Weighted Random Early Detection)
WFQ (Weighted Fair Queuing) and equivalents alongside CAR for differentiated service

front.text: Exam Focus: What is classification?

back.text: Classification is the QoS process responsible for distinguishing one kind of traffic from another by examining the fields in the packet and determining which traffic is to be treated differently. The device performs a lookup and assigns a QoS label to the packet.

front.text: What does the QoS label assigned during classification identify?

back.text: All QoS actions that need to be performed on the packet, and the queue to which the packet is sent. The label is used downstream by scheduling and dropping mechanisms inside the device.

front.text: Match each layer to the parameters used for classification.

rows: IP precedence (IPP), DSCP, IP ECN, source/destination IP

front.text: How does NBAR's deep-packet classification interact with CEF to remain efficient?

back.text: NBAR depends on CEF and performs deep-packet classification only on the FIRST packet of a flow. Once the application is identified, the remainder of packets in that flow are simply CEF-switched — avoiding the cost of repeated deep inspection on every packet.

front.text: What is a PDLM in the context of NBAR?

back.text: PDLM (Packet Description Language Module) is essentially an application signature. NBAR matches application traffic against PDLMs to identify Layer 7 protocols. PDLMs allow nondisruptive updates to recognize new applications without changing the IOS base image.

front.text: Per the ENCOR Exam Alert, at which two layers is QoS marking performed?

rows: IP ToS byte → IPP (3 bits) or DSCP (6 bits)

back.text: Exam Alert: Understand how marking is done at Layer 2 and Layer 3.

front.text: List the recommended IP Precedence (IPP) marking values.

rows: Reserved for network control traffic (e.g., routing)

front.text: Exam Focus: What are the four broad classes of DSCP PHB markings and their RFCs?

back.text:
Best Effort (BE / DSCP 0) | — | Default treatment
Class Selectors (CS1–CS7) | RFC 2474 | Backward compatible with IPP 1–7
Assured Forwarding (AFxy) | RFC 2597 | 4 classes × 3 drop precedences
Expedited Forwarding (EF) | RFC 3268 (commonly RFC 3246) | Low-loss, low-latency

front.text: What does the AFxy notation literally encode?

back.text: x = the AF IP precedence value in decimal (the AF class, 1–4). y = the drop precedence value in decimal (1 = lowest drop precedence, 3 = highest). Example: AF41 combines IP precedence 4 with drop precedence 1.

front.text: Exam Focus: Which RFC defines IP ECN, and where does it live in the ToS byte?

back.text: RFC 3168 defines IP Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN). It uses the last 2 bits of the IP ToS byte — the bits not used by the 6-bit DSCP marking.

front.text: What does it mean for a packet to be 'out of profile' or 'nonconforming'?

back.text: It means the packet exceeded the bandwidth limits defined by a policer. The policer determines, packet-by-packet, whether each packet is in or out of profile and applies a configured action — pass through, drop, or remark.

front.text: Exam Focus: What three actions can a policer's marker perform on a packet?

items: Pass the packet through without modification (conform)
Drop the packet (violate)
Mark down (modify) the assigned DSCP or CoS value and let the packet pass through

front.text: Exam Focus: traffic shaping does not drop traffic — true or false, and what's the trade-off?

back.text: True. Shaping does not drop traffic, so retransmission is not necessary. However, unlike policing, shaping introduces latency (and jitter) because excess packets are buffered until they can be sent.

front.text: Exam Focus: how does shaping handle packets without enough tokens?

back.text: Shaping uses a buffer that ensures packets without enough tokens are buffered rather than dropped immediately. The traffic is held until tokens are available, smoothing the rate to fit within the configured maximum.

front.text: What is the regulatory purpose of shaping in a network design?

back.text: Shaping imposes a maximum rate while regulating traffic so that downstream routers and switches are not subjected to congestion. It limits traffic from a physical or logical interface to fit within a contracted or engineered rate.

front.text: Exam Focus: when are queuing mechanisms activated and deactivated on a device?

back.text: Queuing mechanisms are activated only when a device is experiencing congestion and are deactivated when the congestion no longer exists.

front.text: Per the ENCOR Exam Alert, what should you know about configuring CBWFQ?

back.text: Be able to configure CBWFQ. It defines traffic classes via match criteria (protocols, ACLs, input interfaces), reserves a FIFO queue per class, and assigns each class a guaranteed bandwidth (delivered during congestion), weight, and maximum packet limit.
Exam Alert: Be sure you know how to configure CBWFQ and LLQ.

front.text: Per the ENCOR Exam Alert, what should you know about configuring LLQ?

back.text: LLQ adds a single strict-priority queue to CBWFQ at the class level. You direct traffic for a class to that strict-priority queue using the priority command — providing both bandwidth and latency guarantees, ideal for voice.

policy-map LLQ-EXAMPLE
 class VOICE
  priority percent 10
 class DATA
  bandwidth percent 30

front.text: Exam Focus: what's the difference in scope between queuing algorithms and congestion-avoidance mechanisms?

rows: Drop packets randomly as queue fills, before it overflows

front.text: Exam Focus: what is WRED's role in congestion avoidance?

back.text: WRED is the key congestion-avoidance mechanism in Cisco IOS. It randomly drops packets as queues fill toward capacity, preventing tail drop and TCP global synchronization. Higher-priority traffic has lower drop probability than lower-priority traffic.

front.text: Exam Focus: what are the three broad MQC steps?

items: Define a traffic class using class-map
Create a traffic policy using policy-map (the QoS features within determine how to treat the classified traffic)
Attach the traffic policy to an interface using service-policy

front.text: Exam Focus: true or false — QoS features in a traffic policy determine how to treat the classified traffic.

back.text: True. A traffic class is used to classify traffic, and the QoS features (bandwidth, priority, police, shape, etc.) inside the traffic policy determine how the classified traffic is treated.

front.text: Exam Focus: what does AutoQoS automatically do, and why does it help large deployments?

back.text: AutoQoS automatically matches traffic and assigns matched packets to QoS groups. The output policy map maps specific QoS groups into specific queues (including the priority queue). It enables quick deployment and management of large-scale QoS rollouts using Cisco-validated templates.

front.text: Exam Focus: what standard is WMM based on, and who publishes it?

back.text: WMM (Wi-Fi Multimedia) is a QoS system based on IEEE 802.11e and is published by the Wi-Fi Alliance. Cisco Unified Wireless products support WMM, including UP-to-DSCP mappings, access categories, and user priority.

front.text: Match each Cisco WLC QoS profile to its purpose.
Exam Focus: how can wireless clients be assigned a particular QoS profile?

back.text: By identity through AAA (authentication, authorization, and accounting). The WLAN can be configured with default QoS profiles (Bronze/Silver/Gold/Platinum), and AAA assigns the appropriate profile to a client based on its identity.

front.text: Exam Focus: what is the value range for User Priority (UP) in 802.11e, and what is its bit length?

back.text: 3 bits, with values ranging from 0 to 7. Pairs of UP values are assigned to four Access Categories (ACs) — equating to four distinct service levels over the WLAN.

front.text: Exam Focus: what are the two key outcomes of implementing QoS in a network?

items: More predictable network performance
More effective bandwidth utilization (preferential treatment for important traffic)

front.text: Exam Focus: what does a network device do without QoS configured?

back.text: It offers best-effort service for each packet regardless of contents or size. Packets are sent without any assurance of reliability, delay, or throughput. All traffic competes equally for available resources.

front.text: Exam Focus: how does marking establish a trust boundary?

back.text: Marking (or remarking) sets an attribute of a frame or packet to a specific value. That value establishes a trust boundary — the point in the network where downstream scheduling tools depend on those markings as authoritative for QoS decisions, without needing to reclassify.

front.text: List the seven logical stages a packet goes through in a QoS-aware forwarding path.

items: Classification — identify the traffic type
Marking — write a DSCP/CoS label
Queue assignment — map the marking to an output queue
Scheduling — choose which queue to service next
Congestion detection — track queue depth
Congestion management — drop or throttle when depth grows
Drops — tail drop or WRED action when buffer is exhausted

front.text: Why is classification done first and marking immediately after at the network edge?

back.text: Deep classification is expensive — it inspects multiple header fields or even payload. Doing it once at the edge and immediately marking the packet means downstream devices read a single DSCP/CoS field instead of repeating the costly classification on every hop.

front.text: How does the marking stage feed the queue assignment stage on an egress interface?

back.text: The egress class-map matches on the marked field (commonly DSCP). When a packet arrives at egress, its DSCP is read and matched to a class. The matching class's policy-map action determines which physical/logical queue the packet is placed in (e.g., LLQ priority queue, CBWFQ class queue, or class-default).

front.text: Define queue assignment in the egress QoS pipeline.

back.text: Queue assignment is the step where a classified, marked packet is placed into a specific output queue based on its class. Each policy-map class maps to its own queue (priority queue for LLQ, weighted queue for CBWFQ, FIFO for class-default).

front.text: What is the role of the scheduler in egress QoS?

back.text: The scheduler decides which queue to dequeue from next, and therefore which packet leaves the interface next. It runs continuously — even when the link is uncongested — and uses queue-specific rules (strict-priority, weighted round-robin, etc.) to choose the next packet.

front.text: How does an LLQ scheduler service the priority queue vs CBWFQ classes?

back.text: The LLQ scheduler always checks the priority queue first. If it has packets and is within its policed rate, it dequeues one. Only when the priority queue is empty (or hits its rate limit during congestion) does the scheduler fall back to weighted round-robin among the CBWFQ classes.

front.text: How does the CBWFQ scheduler share bandwidth between classes during congestion?

back.text: CBWFQ uses weighted round-robin scheduling. Each class is assigned a weight derived from its bandwidth statement. During congestion, the scheduler services queues proportionally to those weights, guaranteeing each class at least its configured minimum bandwidth while still allowing unused capacity to be shared.

front.text: When does scheduling actually affect packet ordering?

back.text: Only when more than one queue has packets ready to send AND the link is congested. With a single non-empty queue, packets exit in FIFO order regardless of scheduler. The scheduler's choice only matters when multiple queues compete for the egress link.

front.text: Define egress congestion in QoS terms.

back.text: Egress congestion occurs when the offered traffic rate to an output interface exceeds its transmit capacity. Packets accumulate in egress queues and queue depth grows. QoS scheduling and dropping mechanisms only meaningfully engage during this state.

front.text: What is queue depth and why is it the primary congestion signal?

back.text: Queue depth is the number of packets currently buffered in a queue waiting to be sent. It rises when arrival rate exceeds drain rate (congestion) and falls as the scheduler drains it. Devices use queue depth to trigger congestion-management actions such as WRED drops or ECN marks.

front.text: What does the queue-limit setting on a CBWFQ class control?

back.text: It defines the maximum queue depth (in packets, by default) for that class. Once depth reaches queue-limit, additional arrivals are tail-dropped. Larger queue-limit absorbs more bursts but adds delay; smaller queue-limit keeps latency low but drops sooner.

policy-map WAN
 class BULK
  bandwidth percent 20
  queue-limit 256 packets

front.text: What is the relationship between queue depth and drops?

back.text: Drops are a function of queue depth. Without WRED, drops occur only when depth = queue-limit (tail drop). With WRED, drops begin probabilistically once depth crosses min-threshold and approach 100% as depth nears max-threshold — preventing depth from ever hitting queue-limit.

front.text: Explain tail drop in terms of queue depth and arrivals.

back.text: When queue depth reaches the queue-limit, every newly arriving packet for that queue is dropped at the 'tail' until the scheduler drains enough to free space. No drop probability — it's all-or-nothing once full.

front.text: How does WRED use queue depth to decide drops?

back.text: WRED tracks an exponentially weighted moving average of queue depth. Below min-threshold no packets are dropped; between min and max thresholds, drop probability rises linearly to 1/MPD; above max-threshold all packets are dropped. Higher-priority DSCPs get higher thresholds (dropped later).

front.text: How do the hardware tx-ring and software CBWFQ queues interact at egress?

back.text: Packets normally pass through the tx-ring with no QoS scheduling. When the tx-ring fills (the interface is congested), it pushes back into the software CBWFQ/LLQ queues. The MQC scheduler then arbitrates between class queues. A small tx-ring is essential — too large and software QoS never engages.

front.text: How is the class-default queue scheduled by default in CBWFQ?

back.text: By default, class-default is a single FIFO queue with whatever bandwidth remains after other classes. Adding fair-queue turns it into per-flow WFQ so aggressive flows can't starve others. Adding bandwidth/bandwidth remaining percent gives it a guaranteed share under congestion.

front.text: How many software queues does CBWFQ create on an egress interface?

back.text: One queue per user-defined class, plus the class-default queue, plus the LLQ priority queue if priority is configured. Each class queue is independently sized via queue-limit and independently weighted via bandwidth/bandwidth percent.

front.text: What's the scheduler-level risk of pure strict-priority queuing without LLQ's policer?

back.text: Starvation. A non-stop high-priority flow keeps the scheduler servicing only the priority queue, leaving other queues' depth growing until they tail-drop. LLQ adds an implicit policer that caps the priority queue's rate during congestion, allowing the scheduler to service other classes.

front.text: When does LLQ's implicit priority policer actually engage?

back.text: Only during congestion (when the tx-ring is full and the scheduler is mediating between classes). On an uncongested link, the priority queue can transmit above its configured rate. The moment congestion appears, the policer enforces the configured priority bandwidth, dropping excess priority traffic.

front.text: What does the bandwidth statement in a CBWFQ class actually configure?

back.text: It sets the minimum bandwidth guarantee delivered to the class during congestion. Internally, IOS converts it into a weight used by the WRR scheduler. When uncongested, the class can use more than its bandwidth; when congested, it is guaranteed at least that share.

front.text: Compare bandwidth percent and bandwidth remaining percent in a CBWFQ class.

rows: No LLQ priority class is present
Bandwidth left after priority queue allocation
LLQ is configured — avoids oversubscription errors

front.text: What does it mean if the LLQ priority queue's depth is consistently zero?

back.text: It means the scheduler is dequeuing voice/real-time packets as fast as they arrive — exactly what LLQ is designed to do. Voice traffic experiences minimal queuing delay. Non-zero depth in the priority queue is a red flag for congestion or microbursts hitting the implicit policer.

front.text: show policy-map interface shows a CBWFQ class with queue depth at queue-limit and rising drops. What's the likely fix?

items: Increase the class's bandwidth (or bandwidth remaining percent) so the scheduler drains it faster
Increase queue-limit to absorb bursts (at the cost of latency)
Add WRED so TCP backs off before queue-limit is hit
Verify upstream classification — wrong-class traffic may be flooding this queue

front.text: In show policy-map interface, what's the difference between 'total drops' and 'no-buffer drops' in a class?

rows: Packets dropped because queue depth hit queue-limit or WRED selected them
Packets dropped because the system ran out of buffer memory entirely (uncommon, sizing issue)

front.text: Distinguish congestion management from congestion avoidance.

rows: Activated when congestion exists — prioritizes important traffic
Acts before queue is full — drops/marks selectively to prevent congestion buildup

front.text: Describe the order an LLQ+CBWFQ scheduler considers queues each scheduling cycle.

items: Check priority queue first; dequeue if non-empty and within policed rate
Otherwise, run WRR among CBWFQ class queues using their bandwidth weights
If only class-default has packets, drain it (FIFO or fair-queue if configured)
Repeat continuously

front.text: Why might an egress policy re-mark traffic just before queue assignment?

back.text: To collapse internal classes into a smaller set the next-hop network supports (e.g., enterprise 8-class → SP 4-class), or to mark down policed-but-passed traffic so downstream WRED drops it more aggressively. Re-marking happens after classification but before queue assignment.

front.text: What does egress classification typically match on, and why?

back.text: Egress classification typically matches on DSCP set earlier at the access edge. This keeps egress policies fast (single-field lookup, often hardware-accelerated) and consistent — avoiding repeated NBAR/ACL inspection on every device.

cards.subtopic: Why scheduling can't fix loss without queues

front.text: Why can't a smarter scheduler alone eliminate congestion-related drops?

back.text: The scheduler only chooses what leaves next — it cannot transmit faster than line rate. If sustained arrival exceeds line rate, queue depths grow until queue-limit is reached and drops occur. Scheduling decides who suffers, not whether anyone suffers.

front.text: Show a minimal policy that classifies on DSCP and assigns each class to its own queue with appropriate scheduling.

back.text:
class-map match-any VOICE
 match dscp ef
class-map match-any BUSINESS
 match dscp af31
!
policy-map WAN-OUT
 class VOICE
  priority percent 10
 class BUSINESS
  bandwidth remaining percent 60
  random-detect dscp-based
 class class-default
  bandwidth remaining percent 40
  fair-queue

front.text: Average utilization is 30% but show interface reports steady output drops. What is happening at the queue depth level?

back.text: Microbursts. Short transient spikes drive queue depth to queue-limit faster than show-interface's averaging window can reveal. The scheduler can't drain fast enough during the burst, so tail drops occur. Mitigate with deeper buffers, dedicated class queues, or WRED to throttle TCP earlier.

front.text: Show how to enable DSCP-based WRED on a CBWFQ data class.

back.text:
policy-map WAN-OUT
 class DATA
  bandwidth remaining percent 40
  random-detect dscp-based
  random-detect dscp af11 32 40 10
  random-detect dscp af13 16 40 10
AF13 starts dropping at lower queue depth (min-threshold 16) than AF11 (min-threshold 32) — protecting AF11 traffic during congestion.

front.text: What does the 'Output queue: X/Y/Z' field on show interfaces represent?

rows: Total queue capacity across all classes
Cumulative output drops since last clear

front.text: Describe the egress flow when LLQ + CBWFQ + class-default coexist on one interface.

items: Packet classified by DSCP → marked → assigned to its class queue
Tx-ring full → packets queue in software per-class queues
Scheduler checks priority queue first (LLQ), enforcing rate cap during congestion
Then services CBWFQ classes in WRR proportional to bandwidth weights
class-default drained from remaining bandwidth (FIFO or fair-queue)
Packets exit to tx-ring → onto wire

front.text: What does adding fair-queue under class-default change about scheduling?

back.text: Class-default's single FIFO becomes per-flow WFQ. Within the class-default time slice, the scheduler arbitrates between flows using flow hashes — preventing a few aggressive TCP flows from dominating the bandwidth available to class-default.

front.text: Why is dropping the right action — not 'always bad' — in a well-designed QoS policy?

back.text: Selective drops on lower-priority traffic protect higher-priority traffic. WRED drops scavenger/AFx3 first, signaling TCP to slow down. The result is lower queue depth, lower latency for voice/video, and stable utilization. Eliminating all drops means losing that signaling lever.

front.text: Applying a service-policy fails with 'I/O memory or bandwidth not available'. Why and how is this related to scheduling?

back.text: By default the scheduler reserves 25% of the interface bandwidth for overhead and routing — the sum of CBWFQ bandwidth statements + LLQ priority cannot exceed 75%. Either reduce class allocations, switch to bandwidth remaining percent, or raise max-reserved-bandwidth.

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 max-reserved-bandwidth 90

front.text: How does queue depth in a non-priority class affect voice jitter?

back.text: If voice is in its own LLQ priority queue, other classes' depth is irrelevant — the priority queue is serviced first. But if voice is misclassified into a CBWFQ class, its queueing delay varies with depth, producing jitter. Correct queue assignment is the single biggest factor in voice jitter.

front.text: How does an egress shaper relate to CBWFQ/LLQ scheduling?

back.text: A parent shaper enforces an aggregate output rate (e.g., contracted CIR). When the shaper buffers traffic, it creates artificial congestion — that's exactly when the child policy's CBWFQ/LLQ scheduler engages. Without the shaper, on a sub-rate WAN handoff, the child queueing would never trigger because the physical link isn't congested.

front.text: Compare what queue-limit and WRED control inside a CBWFQ class.

back.text: queue-limit is the hard ceiling. WRED is the soft early-drop curve below that ceiling.

front.text: Why do downstream policies classify on DSCP rather than re-running NBAR?

back.text: DSCP is a fixed-position 6-bit field that hardware can match in a single ACL/TCAM lookup. NBAR requires deep packet inspection and CPU. By trusting DSCP marked at the edge, the rest of the network gets fast, hardware-accelerated classification.

front.text: What is the default queueing discipline INSIDE a single CBWFQ class queue?

back.text: FIFO. Within a single class queue, packets exit in arrival order. The scheduler chooses between class queues using WRR, but inside any one queue there's no fairness — unless fair-queue is added (currently only supported on class-default).

front.text: Which command shows current per-class queue depth and live drops on a router?

back.text: show policy-map interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0
Look for '(queue depth/total drops/no-buffer drops)' under each class — that triplet is the live queue depth, cumulative drops, and buffer-exhaustion drops.

front.text: Trace what happens to an EF-marked voice packet at a congested egress with LLQ configured.

items: Egress classification matches DSCP EF → assigned to VOICE class
Queue assignment: placed into the LLQ strict-priority queue
Tx-ring full (link congested) → scheduler engages
Scheduler services priority queue first; packet exits before any CBWFQ class
If VOICE class is within its policed rate, packet is transmitted with minimal delay
If voice is bursting beyond the priority rate during congestion, the implicit policer drops the excess

front.text: Voice is marked EF at the phone but lands in class-default at the WAN router. Where's the failure in the pipeline?

back.text: Either the access port is untrusted (re-marking EF→0), the WAN egress class-map doesn't match dscp ef, or an intermediate device re-marked the packet. Verify with show policy-map interface — if VOICE class shows zero matches but class-default shows voice volume, classification is the broken stage.

front.text: Why are queueing actions (priority, bandwidth, queue-limit, fair-queue) only valid on egress?

back.text: Queueing manages contention for an egress link's bandwidth. Ingress traffic hasn't yet competed for an output interface, so there's nothing to schedule. Cisco IOS rejects priority/bandwidth/queue-limit on input service-policies for this reason — only classify, mark, and police are valid at ingress.

front.text: How do AF drop-precedence values translate into actual queue behavior?

back.text: AFx1, AFx2, AFx3 share the same class queue (same scheduler weight). WRED gives each a different min-threshold: AFx3 starts dropping at low queue depth, AFx2 a bit higher, AFx1 highest. So under congestion the queue 'sheds' AFx3 first, protecting AFx1's effective throughput.

front.text: How does ECN change the WRED action when queue depth crosses thresholds?

back.text: With WRED ECN enabled, a packet selected by WRED that is ECN-capable (ECT bit set) has its CE bit set instead of being dropped. The receiver echoes congestion to the sender via TCP, which slows down — same effect as a drop but without retransmission. Non-ECN packets are still dropped.

front.text: How do you correlate drops to the QoS pipeline stage that caused them?

rows: show policy-map interface — random-detect drops per DSCP
show policy-map interface — total drops with depth at queue-limit
show policy-map interface — no-buffer drops counter

front.text: Summarize the egress QoS pipeline in one sentence each.

items: Classification — figure out what kind of traffic the packet is
Marking — write that decision into DSCP/CoS so others can reuse it
Queue assignment — place the packet in the right output queue
Scheduling — choose which queue's packet exits next
Congestion (queue depth growing) — the trigger for QoS to actually engage
Congestion management — CBWFQ/LLQ guarantee bandwidth and latency per class
Drops — selective (WRED) or absolute (tail) when depth exceeds the queue's capacity

front.text:
A CBWFQ class has WRED configured with the following thresholds:

AF31: min 8 max 10
AF32: min 5 max 10
AF33: min 2 max 6

Which traffic is most likely to be dropped first during congestion, and why?

back.text:
AF33 traffic is most likely to be dropped first because it has the most aggressive WRED thresholds.

WRED uses average queue depth to begin probabilistic packet drops before the queue is full. Lower minimum thresholds cause earlier drops.

AF drop precedence behavior:
- AF31 = lowest drop precedence (most protected)
- AF32 = medium drop precedence
- AF33 = highest drop precedence (least protected)

In this example:
- AF33 begins random drops at queue depth 2
- AF32 begins random drops at queue depth 5
- AF31 begins random drops at queue depth 8

This demonstrates differentiated packet discard within a single AF queue.

front.text:
A CBWFQ class is configured with:

queue-limit 10 packets
shape average 256000

Two traffic tests are performed:

Test A:
10 packets × 100 bytes

Test B:
10 packets × 1400 bytes

Both queues reach the maximum queue-limit.

Which test introduces more queue delay, and why?

back.text:
Test B introduces significantly more queue delay because larger packets create greater byte depth and require more serialization time.

Important concept:
queue-limit counts packets, not bytes.

Approximate queued data:

Test A:
10 × 100-byte packets = 1,000 bytes

Test B:
10 × 1400-byte packets = 14,000 bytes

At a shaped rate of 256 kbps:
- Larger byte depth takes longer to transmit
- Larger packets consume more tokens from the shaper
- Serialization delay increases dramatically

Key CCNP insight:
The same packet-count queue depth can represent very different actual delay depending on packet size.

front.text:
A network engineer believes AF31, AF32, and AF33 each use separate queues because they have different WRED thresholds.

Why is this assumption incorrect?

back.text:
AF31, AF32, and AF33 do not necessarily represent separate queues.

In a typical CBWFQ + DSCP-based WRED design, they are usually different DSCP values sharing the SAME CBWFQ class queue.

The distinction is:
- One queue exists for the class (for example, cm-af3)
- Different WRED thresholds are applied within that shared queue based on DSCP value

Operationally:
- AF31 receives the most protection
- AF32 is dropped earlier
- AF33 is dropped most aggressively

This allows differentiated packet discard inside a single forwarding class.

Key CCNP insight:
WRED profiles can differ per DSCP while still operating within the same queue.

front.text: Tail Drop vs WRED
Compare tail drop and WRED behavior in a CBWFQ queue.

rows: Yes when dscp-based WRED is enabled

back.text: Key CCNP concept: WRED is designed to influence TCP behavior before complete queue collapse occurs.

Interpret the output. What does it prove about congestion handling inside the AF3 class?

WRED is operational because random drops are occurring.

front.text:
Place the following shaping events in the correct operational order:

- Packet transmitted
- Packet arrives
- Packet queued
- Tokens replenished
- Shaper checks token availability

items: Packet arrives
Shaper checks token availability
If insufficient tokens exist, packet queued
Tokens replenished over time
Packet transmitted once sufficient tokens exist

back.text: Packet -> Token Check -> Queue (if needed) -> Tokens Refill -> Transmission
Key CCNP concept: shaping delays packets rather than immediately dropping them.

front.text:
Class-map: class-default
  shape (average) cir 256000, bc 1024, be 1024
  target shape rate 256000
Interpret the output. What operational behavior should occur on this interface?

items: Traffic is shaped to an average rate of 256 kbps.
Packets exceeding the current token availability are queued rather than immediately dropped.
The shaper uses a token bucket mechanism with Bc and Be values controlling burst behavior.
Congestion is intentionally created so child CBWFQ queues become active.
Queue depth and delay increase when ingress traffic exceeds 256 kbps.

back.text: Key CCNP insight: shaping delays packets; policing drops packets.

A CBWFQ class has a queue-limit of 10 packets.

Two flows enter the queue:
- Flow A: 10 packets at 100 bytes each
- Flow B: 10 packets at 1500 bytes each

Both queues are full.

Which flow creates more serialization delay and why?

back.text: Queue-limit counts packets, not bytes. Larger packets consume more serialization time and represent deeper byte queues.

front.text:
Misconception Trap:

AF31, AF32, and AF33 must each use separate hardware queues because they have different WRED thresholds.
Why is this assumption incorrect?

back.text:
AF31, AF32, and AF33 commonly share the SAME CBWFQ class queue while using DIFFERENT WRED drop profiles.

The queue is associated to the class (for example, cm-af3), while WRED thresholds are associated to DSCP values within that queue.

cm-af3 queue
 ├── AF31 → higher thresholds
 ├── AF32 → medium thresholds
 └── AF33 → aggressive thresholds

items: How quickly average queue depth reacts to congestion
How smooth the moving average calculation becomes
How sensitive WRED is to short bursts

AF33 min/max = 2/6

Why is this expected?

AF drop precedence controls which traffic becomes disposable first during congestion.

front.text: What is the most important operational misconception about 'queue-limit 10 packets'?

back.text:
A queue-limit of 10 packets does NOT represent a fixed amount of delay.

Actual delay depends heavily on packet size and transmission rate.

front.text: When does tail drop occur in a queue?

items: Packets continue entering queue until queue-limit is reached.
Once queue is full, new arriving packets are discarded.
Tail drop is reactive because it waits for full queue exhaustion.

Why is mean queue depth important to WRED operation?

back.text:
WRED decisions are based on average queue depth rather than instantaneous queue depth.

This prevents short microbursts from immediately triggering aggressive packet drops.

back.text: WRED begins probabilistic packet drops before the queue becomes completely full, attempting to signal congestion early and prevent queue collapse.

front.text: Why must wireless clients be authenticated before being granted access to a wireless network?

back.text: To control access so that only trusted/expected devices reach corporate or sensitive resources. Guests should be limited to a separate WLAN, and rogue or unknown clients should be blocked from associating at all. Authentication validates either the device, the user, or both before network access is granted.

front.text: What is Open Authentication?

back.text: Open Authentication is the original 802.11 method that requires no credentials. The client sends an 802.11 authentication request and is allowed to associate. It validates only that the client is a valid 802.11 device — not the user's identity. Real user authentication must be added through other means (e.g., WebAuth).

front.text: Where is Open Authentication most commonly seen in practice?

back.text: On public/guest WLANs at hotels, coffee shops, airports, etc. Clients associate immediately, then a WebAuth portal typically prompts them to accept an Acceptable Use Policy or enter basic credentials before full network access is granted.

front.text: On an IOS XE WLC, where do you configure Open Authentication on a WLAN, and what setting indicates Open Auth?

items: Configuration > Tags & Profiles > WLANs
Add or edit a WLAN; set the SSID under the General tab
Go to Security > Layer 2 tab
Set Layer 2 Security Mode to None

back.text: When verified in the WLAN list, the Security column will show [open].

front.text: Compare WPA, WPA2, and WPA3 at a high level.

rows: Original Wi-Fi Alliance certification; uses TKIP. Now considered weak/deprecated.
Adds AES/CCMP encryption; widely deployed. Personal mode vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks against 4-way handshake.
Most secure; Personal mode replaces PSK exchange with SAE, adds forward secrecy and protection against dictionary attacks.

back.text: Always use the highest WPA version supported by your WLCs, APs, and clients.

front.text: What is the difference between WPA personal mode and WPA enterprise mode?

rows: Small environments, embedded devices; same key configured on all clients and APs
Larger enterprises with per-user credentials and a RADIUS authentication server

front.text: When PSK is used (without ISE), where must the key string be configured?

back.text: On all wireless clients AND on all APs/WLCs (i.e., on the WLAN configuration). Every device using the WLAN must have an identical pre-shared key. The key itself is never transmitted over the air.

front.text: If the PSK is never sent over the air, how do the client and AP agree on encryption keys?

back.text: The PSK is used directly as the Pairwise Master Key (PMK). The client and AP then perform a four-way EAPOL handshake that uses the PMK to derive a Pairwise Transient Key (PTK) for unicast traffic and a Groupwise Transient Key (GTK) for broadcast/multicast traffic. The handshake material can be exchanged in the clear because it cannot be used without the PMK.

front.text: What attack is WPA/WPA2 Personal mode vulnerable to, and how does WPA3 Personal mitigate it?

back.text: WPA/WPA2 Personal is vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks: an attacker captures the 4-way handshake and brute-forces the PSK. With the key, they can decrypt traffic or join the network.

WPA3 Personal replaces the PSK exchange with Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (SAE), which resists offline dictionary attacks and provides forward secrecy — captured traffic cannot be decrypted later, even if the password is compromised.

front.text: What is SAE in WPA3-Personal?

back.text: Simultaneous Authentication of Equals — a key exchange method where the client and AP authenticate as peers (equally and even simultaneously), rather than the client authenticating against the AP. SAE is resistant to offline dictionary attacks and enables forward secrecy.

front.text: What is forward secrecy, and which WPA mode provides it?

back.text: Forward secrecy means that even if the password/key is later compromised, an attacker cannot decrypt previously captured wireless traffic. WPA3-Personal provides forward secrecy; WPA2-Personal does not.

front.text: Outline the IOS XE WLC steps to configure a WLAN for WPA2-Personal (PSK) with AES.

items: Configuration > Tags & Profiles > WLANs > Add
On General tab, set Profile Name and SSID
Security > Layer 2 tab: select WPA+WPA2
Uncheck WPA Policy; check WPA2 Policy and WPA2 Encryption AES (CCMP128)
Under Auth Key Mgmt, check only PSK
Enter the Pre-Shared Key string
Apply to Device, then map WLAN to a Policy profile via a Policy tag and apply to APs

back.text: In the WLAN list, Security should display [WPA2][PSK][AES].

front.text: Exam tip: what are the operational drawbacks of using PSK in a large environment?

items: Every device must be configured with the same key
Changing the key requires touching every device
Key disclosure to one user effectively compromises everyone
No per-user accountability or identity
Vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks (WPA2-PSK)

front.text: Why should hybrid WPA modes (e.g., WPA+WPA2 with TKIP+AES) generally be avoided?

back.text: A WLAN is only as secure as its weakest enabled cipher. Mixing WPA/WPA2 with TKIP weakens overall security and has been deprecated. Hybrid modes can also cause client compatibility issues. Use WPA2 or WPA3 with AES/CCMP only.

front.text: What is EAP?

back.text: Extensible Authentication Protocol — a flexible authentication framework (not a single method). EAP defines common functions used by specific authentication methods (PEAP, EAP-TLS, EAP-FAST, etc.) so that 802.11 itself doesn't need to implement each method natively.

front.text: Name and describe the three 802.1X roles in a wireless deployment.

rows: Controls port/network access; relays auth via EAPOL/RADIUS
Validates credentials against user database/policy and grants/denies access

front.text: When a Cisco WLC is configured for 802.1X with an external RADIUS server, what role does the WLC play?

back.text: The authenticator. It sits between the supplicant (client) and the authentication server (RADIUS), relaying EAP messages over EAPOL toward the client and over RADIUS toward the server, and enforcing access based on the AS's decision.

front.text: In WPA2-Enterprise, where does the supplicant live?

back.text: On the wireless client device. The supplicant is the software (built-in OS supplicant or third-party) that requests authentication and presents credentials to the authenticator.

front.text: What is EAPOL, and where is it used?

back.text: EAP over LAN — the encapsulation used to carry EAP messages over wired and wireless LANs between the supplicant and the authenticator. The authenticator then translates EAP into RADIUS for the authentication server.

front.text: What is the purpose of the EAPOL four-way handshake?

back.text: To exchange and derive dynamic encryption keys between the client and AP after authentication. It does NOT authenticate the user — it derives the PTK (unicast key) and distributes the GTK (broadcast/multicast key) so the wireless link can be encrypted.

front.text: Explain the key hierarchy used to secure WPA2/WPA3 wireless traffic.

items: PMK (Pairwise Master Key) — derived from EAP auth (Enterprise) or directly from the PSK (Personal)
GMK (Groupwise Master Key) — generated on the AP for group key derivation
PTK (Pairwise Transient Key) — derived from PMK during the 4-way handshake; protects unicast traffic
GTK (Groupwise Transient Key) — derived from GMK; distributed via 4-way handshake; protects broadcast/multicast

front.text: List the high-level message exchange of the EAPOL four-way handshake.

items: Message 1: AP → Client — sends ANonce, allowing client to derive PTK
Message 2: Client → AP — sends SNonce + MIC; AP can now derive PTK
Message 3: AP → Client — sends GTK (encrypted with PTK) + MIC; instructs client to install keys
Message 4: Client → AP — acknowledgment that keys are installed

back.text: After all four messages succeed, 802.1X unblocks the port and encrypted traffic can flow.

front.text: Where are specific EAP methods (PEAP, EAP-TLS, EAP-FAST, etc.) configured — on the WLC or somewhere else?

back.text: They are configured on the RADIUS authentication server and must be matched on the client supplicant. The WLC only needs to know that 802.1X is in use; it does not need to know which specific EAP method is being negotiated, since it just relays EAP between client and server.

front.text: Name several common EAP methods supported in WPA Enterprise mode.

items: LEAP (legacy, Cisco proprietary, deprecated)
EAP-FAST
PEAP
EAP-TLS
EAP-TTLS
EAP-SIM

front.text: When configuring 802.1X for a WLAN on IOS XE, what AAA method-list Type do you select, and what server group?

Add a method list (or edit default)
Type: dot1x

front.text: Outline the WLAN-side settings on an IOS XE WLC to enable WPA2-Enterprise with 802.1X.

items: Configuration > Tags & Profiles > WLANs > Add
Security > Layer 2: select WPA+WPA2
Check WPA2 Policy; uncheck WPA Policy

front.text: What is Local EAP on a Cisco WLC, and when would you use it?

back.text: Local EAP allows the WLC itself to act as the authentication server, validating clients against a local user database. It's useful in small deployments or as a fallback when external RADIUS servers are unreachable. In larger networks, an external RADIUS server (e.g., Cisco ISE) is preferred.

front.text: What is WebAuth, and how does it differ from PSK or EAP authentication?

back.text: Web Authentication presents the user with a web page (AUP, login form, splash page) before granting network access. Unlike PSK or EAP, WebAuth is interactive — it directly engages the end user via a browser. It's commonly added on top of Open Authentication for guest networks.

front.text: Guests must read and accept an Acceptable Use Policy before getting network access. Which authentication method natively supports this?

back.text: WebAuth. It can present an AUP page in the user's browser; access is granted only after the user acknowledges/accepts. Open Authentication, WPA2/WPA3 PSK, and WPA Enterprise alone don't natively present user-facing content.

front.text: List the Local Web Authentication (LWA) modes available on a Cisco WLC.

items: LWA with internal database on the WLC
LWA with external database (RADIUS or LDAP)
LWA with external redirect after authentication
LWA with external splash page redirect, using internal DB on WLC
LWA with passthrough (user acknowledgment only)

front.text: Compare Local Web Authentication (LWA) and Central Web Authentication (CWA).

rows: Hosted on a central server (e.g., Cisco ISE)
Many WLCs with centralized policy and user DB

front.text: Outline the IOS XE steps to enable WebAuth on a WLAN.

items: Configuration > Security > WebAuth: create a parameter map (e.g., MyWebAuth) with banner/redirect/customization
Configuration > Security > AAA > AAA Method List: create a list of Type 'login' assigned to RADIUS server group (e.g., webauth)
Configuration > Tags & Profiles > WLANs: add WLAN; set SSID on General tab
Security > Layer 2: configure Layer 2 settings (often None for guest)
Security > Layer 3: enable WebAuth, select the parameter map and the login authentication list
Apply to Device, link WLAN profile to a Policy profile via a Policy tag, apply to APs

back.text: In the WLAN list, the Security column will show [Web_Auth].

front.text: Exam tip: which AAA method-list Type is used for 802.1X versus WebAuth?
Can WebAuth be combined with other authentication methods?

back.text: Yes. WebAuth is a Layer 3 mechanism and can be layered on top of Open Authentication, PSK, or EAP-based authentication. For example, a guest WLAN may use Open Authentication at Layer 2 plus WebAuth at Layer 3 to display an AUP and capture credentials.

front.text: How do you verify which security method a WLAN is configured for in IOS XE WLC GUI, and what values appear?

back.text: Configuration > Tags & Profiles > WLANs lists all WLANs and a Security column. Typical values:

front.text: Clients on a WPA2-Enterprise WLAN cannot authenticate. The WLC shows RADIUS timeouts. What are the most likely causes to check?

items: RADIUS server reachability from the WLC management/source interface (ping/route)
RADIUS shared secret mismatch between WLC and server
Wrong RADIUS auth/acct port numbers
WLC's NAD/source IP not configured as a network device on RADIUS (e.g., ISE)
ACL or firewall blocking UDP 1812/1813 between WLC and RADIUS
Wrong AAA method list referenced under WLAN Security > AAA

front.text: A client repeatedly fails to associate with a WPA2-PSK WLAN and never reaches IP connectivity. What is the most common cause?

back.text: PSK mismatch between client and WLAN configuration. The 4-way handshake fails because the PMK derived on each side doesn't match. Re-enter the PSK on the client and confirm it matches the WLAN configuration exactly (case-sensitive, no extra whitespace).

front.text: Recommend an authentication design for: (a) corporate employees, (b) IoT/embedded sensors, (c) guests.

rows: WPA2/WPA3 Enterprise (802.1X) with EAP-TLS or PEAP, RADIUS (ISE) for per-user identity
WPA2/WPA3 Personal (PSK), preferably PSK with ISE (iPSK) for unique per-device keys
Open Authentication + WebAuth (LWA or CWA) with AUP and optional sponsor/credential login

front.text: Quick exam recall: summarize the four wireless authentication methods covered in Chapter 20.

rows: Public/guest WLANs, often combined with WebAuth
